entries.title: Scenario name
entries.what: A short human label for this sizing exercise, like Support agent pilot or Claims triage rollout. It names the saved scenario, the report header, and every export so two engagements never get confused.
entries.why: Sizing work is iterative and Customers usually end up with several scenarios compared against each other. A stable name is what lets you compare the pilot against the full rollout honestly instead of overwriting one guess with another.
entries.math: No formula consumes it and no number moves when it changes. It appears in the exported report title and scenario snapshots, and loading a preset overwrites it with the preset label. Every computed value from monthlyRuns to ceilingMonthly stays identical.

entries.title: Customer name
entries.what: Who this scenario is for. It defaults to Customer A on purpose so exports stay anonymous until you deliberately decide otherwise.
entries.why: Sizing documents travel into email, decks, and shared drives, and a real Customer name in a forwarded file is a confidentiality incident waiting to happen. TokenOps assumes the safe default and warns loudly when a real name is present.
entries.math: No formula reads it. Any value other than Customer A trips the customerNameWarning check on every export, and the sanitized export path resets it to Customer A along with industry and process. Cost and sizing outputs are unaffected.

entries.title: Industry
entries.what: The Customer's vertical in your own words: legal services, health benefits administration, telecom. It is free text because real verticals rarely fit clean dropdown bins.
entries.why: Industry sets the language of the report and the risks worth naming. A claims processing agent and a marketing copy agent can have identical token math and completely different governance stories, and the reader needs to know which one they are holding.
entries.math: It enters no cost or sizing formula. The recommend function lists a blank industry in missingData, so leaving it empty keeps report language generic and visibly lowers the completeness of the recommendation. Route scores and totals do not move.

entries.title: Customer size
entries.what: A rough bucket for organizational scale: SMB, mid market, or enterprise. It signals procurement style, security review depth, and how much operational muscle exists to run infrastructure.
entries.why: The same workload lands differently at 80 people versus 40,000. An enterprise can amortize private hardware and staff it around the clock; an SMB usually cannot, no matter how attractive the ownership math looks on paper.
entries.math: It does not enter the token or cost formulas; scale reaches the math through users, adoptionPercent, and monthlyRuns instead. It is recorded in the scenario summary and export so a reader can sanity check whether the volume inputs actually match the size claim.

entries.title: Primary business process
entries.what: The actual process the AI touches: Customer support, contract review, prior authorization. One named process per scenario keeps the sizing honest.
entries.why: AI for the company is unsizable; AI for tier 1 support tickets is sizable. Naming the process moves the conversation from technology shopping to workload definition, which is where every numeric input below gets its value.
entries.math: No formula consumes it and the sanitized export blanks it. Its influence is indirect but real: a concrete process makes runsPerUserPerDay, topology, and adoption answers factual instead of invented, and those feed monthlyRuns directly.

entries.title: Budget confidence
entries.what: How real the money is: unknown, low, medium, or high. It is a signal about the engagement, not a dollar figure.
entries.why: Sizing an unfunded project produces a number nobody can act on, and worse, a number that gets quoted later out of context. TokenOps refuses to pretend: with no budget signal the honest deliverable is discovery questions, not a spreadsheet.
entries.math: checkDoNotSize treats unknown budget confidence as a missing critical gate, which flips the entire recommendation to Do not size yet regardless of every other input. Move it to low or better and scoreRoutes runs normally and the full formula chain lights up.

entries.title: Existing cloud preference
entries.what: The cloud the Customer already lives in, if any: Azure, AWS, Google, or none. Free text because reality includes multicloud estates and inherited contracts.
entries.why: An incumbent cloud carries committed spend agreements, security reviews already passed, and billing paths already approved. A model served through that cloud often wins on friction even when raw token prices are similar elsewhere.
entries.math: In scoreRoutes any nonempty value adds the cloudPreferenceMatch bonus to the cloud model service route. It never changes token counts or the provider totals in roleRoutedCost; only the route ranking shifts.

entries.title: Main driver
entries.what: The single strongest reason this project exists, in the Customer's own words: cost pressure, a backlog they cannot staff, a compliance deadline, competitive fear.
entries.why: Every recommendation reads differently depending on the driver. A cost driven Customer needs the ceiling math up front; a speed driven Customer needs time to value first. Writing it down anchors the report to their motivation instead of yours.
entries.math: Narrative only; no formula consumes it and the sanitized export clears it. If the driver is cost, the downstream fields that matter most are savingsThresholdPercent and the ceilingMonthly card it produces.

entries.title: Main risk
entries.what: The failure mode the Customer fears most: wrong answers reaching the public, data leakage, runaway spend, a rollout that stalls.
entries.why: Risk shapes route choice as much as need does. Leakage fear pushes toward private execution, cost fear pushes toward caching and the budget ceiling, and quality fear pushes toward judge roles and human approval gates.
entries.math: No direct numeric effect; it is report language. It should agree with the policy gate though: a stated leakage risk alongside dataCanLeave set to yes is a contradiction any careful reader will catch, and the scoring will not hide it for you.

entries.title: Can data leave the Customer environment?
entries.what: The master policy question: may Customer data cross the boundary into a third party service at all? The answers are yes, only with controls, or no.
entries.why: This single answer sorts the whole solution space before any economics run. If data cannot leave, every public endpoint is off the table no matter how cheap its tokens are, and the real conversation becomes private serving and hardware.
entries.math: It is the largest input to privatePolicyScore: no earns the dataCannotLeave points and with controls earns a smaller share, and that score multiplies into private route scores while penalizing the direct route in scoreRoutes. It is also a checkDoNotSize gate, and a public route leading while this says no fires a critical policy conflict warning.

entries.title: Can prompts leave?
entries.what: Whether the text users type, plus the system instructions wrapped around it, may be sent to an external model API. Prompts routinely contain names, account numbers, and internal project detail.
entries.why: Teams often approve the model without noticing that prompts are the data. Splitting this question out forces the security review to look at what actually crosses the wire on every single call, not just where files are stored.
entries.math: No score weight reads it directly today; policy pressure enters through dataCanLeave and privatePolicyScore. The quick start flow keeps it consistent, forcing it to no when data cannot leave, and it is recorded in the policy summary of every export so an inconsistent answer stays visible.

entries.title: Can retrieved documents leave?
entries.what: Whether retrieved documents, the chunks a RAG pipeline injects into prompts, may leave the environment. In a RAG system the documents are the payload and are usually far larger than the user's question.
entries.why: Retrieval quietly turns a chat request into a bulk document export. A Customer comfortable sending questions to a provider may be very uncomfortable sending contract text along with each one, and this field makes that distinction explicit before architecture is chosen.
entries.math: It carries no direct score weight; dataCanLeave carries the points into privatePolicyScore. Its cost relevance is contextual: the retrievedContextTokens each query injects is exactly the material this answer governs, and that volume flows into effectiveRolePlan as worker input.

entries.title: Can embeddings leave?
entries.what: Whether document embeddings, the numeric vectors made from your text for similarity search, may be computed or stored by an outside service. Embeddings are derived data, not plaintext, but they are not anonymous either.
entries.why: Embedding inversion research shows vectors can leak substantial source text, so serious reviews treat embeddings as data rather than exhaust. The answer decides whether a hosted vector database and a hosted embedding API are even candidates.
entries.math: No route weight consumes it directly; it documents posture in the export. The workload it governs is the monthlyEmbeddingTokens stream priced through embeddingPricePerMillion inside roleRoutedCost, all of which stays inside the environment if this is no.

entries.title: Data sensitivity
entries.what: A blunt classification of the data this workload touches: low, medium, or high. High means material like health records, financials, or privileged legal documents.
entries.why: Sensitivity sets the blast radius of a mistake. Two workloads with identical volume deserve different routes when one handles cafeteria menus and the other handles patient claims, and this field is where that difference enters the model.
entries.math: A high setting adds the highSensitivity points inside privatePolicyScore, raising the policy pressure that scoreRoutes applies for private routes and against the direct route. Low and medium add nothing, and the field never touches token counts or cost.

entries.title: Regulated data present
entries.what: A flag for data under a specific legal regime: HIPAA, GDPR, PCI, CMMC, and their relatives. Regulated is narrower than sensitive; it means an external auditor has an enforceable opinion.
entries.why: Regulation converts preferences into requirements. Once regulated data is in scope, provider selection becomes a compliance exercise with evidence and attestations, not a pricing comparison, and the sizing must respect that.
entries.math: When on it adds the regulatedData points to privatePolicyScore, pushing private execution routes up the ranking in scoreRoutes. It has no effect on token counts or the provider totals in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Data residency required
entries.what: Whether the data must stay within a specific country or legal jurisdiction. Residency is about the geography of processing and storage, not about who the vendor is.
entries.why: Residency rules can disqualify a provider's default endpoints while permitting the same provider's regional or sovereign offering. It is one of the most common silent dealbreakers in enterprise AI procurement, so it must be asked early.
entries.math: It adds residencyRequired points to privatePolicyScore, applies the residencyPenalty against the direct route, and adds the residencyFriendly bonus to the cloud route in scoreRoutes, since hyperscalers offer regional deployments that direct APIs may not.

entries.title: Private networking required
entries.what: Whether traffic to the model must ride private links, private endpoints, or VPC peering rather than the public internet. This is transport isolation, distinct from where data rests or who processes it.
entries.why: Many enterprises accept an external processor but forbid the public internet path to it. The requirement shapes network architecture and quietly shortens the vendor shortlist to those offering private connectivity.
entries.math: It carries no score weight in scoreRoutes today and no cost effect; it is recorded in the policy summary and export. Treat it as a hard architectural constraint to verify against whichever route the scoring recommends.

entries.title: Air gap required
entries.what: The strictest isolation posture: the system must run with no network path to the outside world at all. Common in defense, classified, and some industrial control environments.
entries.why: An air gap eliminates every hosted option in one stroke, including private cloud. Only hardware inside the gap can serve the model, so this flag reshapes the entire recommendation before any economics are consulted.
entries.math: It adds airGapRequired points to privatePolicyScore and, together with requiresOnPrem, fires the strictPrivateExecution penalty in scoreRoutes against orchestration platforms that assume hosted reach. Cost formulas are untouched; feasibility is what changes.

entries.title: Human approval required for actions
entries.what: Whether a human must approve agent actions before they take effect, especially writes into systems of record. This is the classic human in the loop control.
entries.why: Approval gates are the difference between an agent that drafts and an agent that acts. For regulated processes they are often the specific control that makes deployment approvable at all, so the answer belongs in the sizing record.
entries.math: No score weight or token formula reads it; leaving it off adds an approval question to discoveryQuestions. Operationally an approval gate caps effective automation speed, which is worth remembering when you set runsPerUserPerDay upstream of monthlyRuns.

entries.title: Audit trail required
entries.what: Whether every agent interaction must land in a reviewable, retained record: prompts, outputs, tool calls, and decisions. It is both a governance control and a genuine storage workload.
entries.why: Audit is how AI systems earn trust with compliance teams. If nobody can reconstruct what the agent did and why, regulated Customers cannot run it, and incident response has nothing to work with.
entries.math: When on it adds auditTrailRequired points to privatePolicyScore, nudging governed and private routes upward in scoreRoutes. The storage consequence is computed separately in the memory section, where traceRetained, traceRetentionDays, and avgBytesPerTokenInTrace turn tokens into disk.

entries.title: Customer requires on premises
entries.what: A hard statement that the workload must run on hardware the Customer controls, in their facility or colocation. This is a requirement, not a preference.
entries.why: Some Customers have policy, contractual, or sovereignty reasons that no cloud attestation can satisfy. Learning this early prevents weeks spent pricing routes that were never actually permitted.
entries.math: In scoreRoutes it fires the privateExecutionHard weight for private platform routes and contributes to the strictPrivateExecution penalty against hosted orchestration; the quick start flow also keeps it consistent with dataCanLeave. Economics then hinge on ceilingMonthly and the gpusRequiredByThroughput sizing gates rather than provider price lists.

entries.title: Customer permits private cloud
entries.what: Whether a dedicated, single tenant cloud deployment is acceptable: private cloud or a hosted private stack. It is the middle ground between public APIs and owned iron.
entries.why: Many Customers who refuse shared public endpoints will accept isolated infrastructure operated by someone else. That opens routes with private execution but without the capital and staffing burden of full ownership.
entries.math: When requiresOnPrem is off, this flag fires the privateExecutionSoft weight for private platform routes in scoreRoutes; the hard and soft weights are mutually exclusive so only the applicable one scores. No token or cost formula reads it.

entries.title: Customer permits public cloud
entries.what: Whether public cloud hosted model services are allowed at all. This is the permission side of the policy posture, complementing the restrictions above it.
entries.why: An explicit yes or no here prevents the classic failure where a team designs around a hyperscaler and security vetoes it in week nine. A permission written down is a permission someone can be held to.
entries.math: No route weight consumes it directly; the quick start flow forces it off when dataCanLeave is no so the posture stays internally consistent. Its practical effect is to validate or invalidate the direct and cloud routes that scoreRoutes may rank highly.

entries.title: Customer requires marketplace billing
entries.what: Whether purchases must flow through a cloud marketplace so AI spend lands on the existing Azure, AWS, or Google bill. This is a procurement constraint, not a technical one.
entries.why: Marketplace transactions can draw down committed spend agreements and skip new vendor onboarding entirely, compressing procurement from months to days. For some Customers that friction difference decides between otherwise equivalent routes.
entries.math: When on it adds the marketplaceBilling bonus to the cloud model service route in scoreRoutes. Provider token prices and the totals in roleRoutedCost are unaffected; only the route ranking moves.

entries.title: Modern agent workload (deep path)
entries.what: The deep sizing path: an agent workload modeled from first principles with roles, calls per run, and tokens per call, instead of one flat heuristic. Turning it on reveals the volume, topology, anatomy, snowball, and routing sections.
entries.why: Agent systems multiply model calls in ways a single estimate cannot capture; a planner, six workers, and a judge is a different animal from a chatbot. The deep path exists so those multipliers are visible before they appear on an invoice.
entries.math: It activates the chain monthlyRuns, baseCallsPerRun, totalCallsPerRun, then inputTokensPerRun and outputTokensPerRun through effectiveRolePlan, and those agent tokens are priced per role by roleRoutedCost. Everything sums with the quick workloads in totalMonthlyTokens.

entries.title: RAG assistant (quick formula)
entries.what: A quick field formula for a retrieval augmented chat assistant: people asking questions against indexed documents during working hours. It sizes from concurrency and schedule rather than per call anatomy.
entries.why: RAG assistants are usually the first AI workload an organization deploys, and a defensible ballpark in two minutes beats a perfect model next quarter. Use this early, then graduate to the deep path once telemetry exists.
entries.math: It enables ragMonthlyTokens: tokens per session minute times concurrentConnections times ragDays times ragHours times 60. The result flows into totalMonthlyTokens and is priced at the worker role rate with the quickInputSharePercent split inside roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Always-on agents (quick formula)
entries.what: A quick formula for always on agentic workflows: unattended pipelines that monitor, process, and act on a schedule rather than waiting for a human. Sized by workflow count and active hours.
entries.why: Steady background agents are token furnaces precisely because nobody watches them run; the burn is constant while human attention is not. A dedicated line item keeps them from hiding inside the total.
entries.math: It enables agentsMonthlyTokens: tokens per workflow minute times workflows times agDays times agHours times 60. The result joins totalMonthlyTokens, adds its active window to weightedTokensPerMinute, and is priced at the worker rate in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Coding assistant (quick formula)
entries.what: A quick formula for interactive coding assistants: developers using AI completion and chat inside their editors. It sizes from headcount and active hours with a field heuristic near 91 thousand tokens per developer hour.
entries.why: Developer tooling is often the second AI bill a company discovers, and it scales linearly with the engineers who actually use it. Counting it separately keeps developer demand from vanishing into the general assistant estimate.
entries.math: It enables codingMonthlyTokens: tokens per developer hour times developers times codDays times codHours. The result sums into totalMonthlyTokens and prices at the worker rate with the quickInputSharePercent split in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Agentic coding (quick formula)
entries.what: A quick formula for autonomous coding agents: systems that plan changes, edit files, run tests, read failures, and iterate with limited supervision. The heuristic burn is higher than assistant style coding, near 104 thousand tokens per developer hour.
entries.why: The tool loop is the difference: every test run and error message reenters the context, so agentic coding consumes far more than autocomplete ever will. Budgets built on assistant era numbers get surprised exactly here.
entries.math: It enables agenticCodingMonthlyTokens: tokens per developer hour times acDevelopers times acDays times acHours. The result joins totalMonthlyTokens and the worker rate quick pricing block of roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Custom workload tokens per month
entries.what: A raw monthly token number for anything the built in shapes do not cover: a workload measured from telemetry, a vendor supplied estimate, or a migration whose size you already know. Entered directly in tokens per month.
entries.why: Real engagements always contain one workload that fits no template, and the honest move is to take a measured number as is rather than force it through someone else's heuristic. It is also the fastest path when the Customer already has usage data.
entries.math: It adds directly into totalMonthlyTokens with no transformation and is priced at the worker role rate using the quickInputSharePercent split in roleRoutedCost. A positive value also counts as known usage volume for the checkDoNotSize gate.

entries.title: Quick workload input share percent
entries.what: The split applied to all quick formula tokens between input, what the model reads, and output, what it writes. Seventy percent means 70 of every 100 quick tokens bill at the input rate and 30 at the output rate.
entries.why: Providers price output tokens several times higher than input, so an undifferentiated lump of tokens cannot be priced without assuming a split. This field makes that assumption visible and editable instead of buried in someone's spreadsheet.
entries.math: Inside the quick workload block of roleRoutedCost this share sets the input portion, cachedInputPercent then discounts part of that input, and the remainder bills as output. Lowering the share raises cost because more tokens land on the expensive output rate.

entries.title: RAG concurrent connections
entries.what: How many RAG sessions are active at the same moment during working hours, not how many users hold accounts. Ten thousand licensed users might mean forty truly concurrent sessions.
entries.why: Concurrency is what serving capacity actually feels; monthly totals are what invoices feel. Confusing concurrent sessions with total users inflates the token estimate by orders of magnitude, the single most common quick sizing error.
entries.math: It multiplies inside ragMonthlyTokens alongside tokens per session minute, ragDays, and ragHours. A positive value also satisfies the usage volume gate in checkDoNotSize for scenarios where RAG is the only enabled workload.

entries.title: RAG days per month
entries.what: How many days per month the RAG assistant sees real use. Office workloads cluster near 21 or 22 working days; consumer facing services run all 30.
entries.why: The active window is the difference between a business tool and an always on service, and the bill follows the window. Defaulting everything to 30 quietly overstates office workloads by roughly a third.
entries.math: A direct multiplier in ragMonthlyTokens, and a contributor of active minutes to the denominator of weightedTokensPerMinute. Raising it increases monthly tokens while spreading demand across a wider window.

entries.title: RAG hours per day
entries.what: Active hours per day for the RAG workload. A single region office runs 8 to 10; global follow the sun support can justify 24.
entries.why: Hours per day is the second half of the active window and the easiest place to double count with concurrency. If concurrentConnections already reflects the busiest hour, keep hours honest to the true span of daily usage.
entries.math: Multiplies inside ragMonthlyTokens with ragDays and concurrentConnections, and adds to the active minutes behind weightedTokensPerMinute, the demand rate downstream sizing treats as what the hardware must sustain.

entries.title: Agentic workflows
entries.what: The count of distinct always on agentic workflows running: a ticket triager, an invoice processor, and a monitoring summarizer count as three. Provisioned per pipeline, not per person.
entries.why: Unattended workflows scale with automation ambition, so headcount tells you nothing here. Counting pipelines is how you catch the quiet automation sprawl that dominates some Customers' token bills.
entries.math: A direct multiplier in agentsMonthlyTokens: tokens per workflow minute times workflows times agDays times agHours times 60. A positive count also satisfies the checkDoNotSize usage gate when agents are the only enabled workload.

entries.title: Agent days per month
entries.what: Days per month the always on workflows actually run. Business calendar pipelines run about 22; true continuous automation runs all 30 or 31.
entries.why: Software does not take weekends unless told to, so agent days should default higher than human workload days. Getting this wrong understates the steadiest, most predictable slice of total demand.
entries.math: Multiplies inside agentsMonthlyTokens and contributes active minutes to weightedTokensPerMinute. For genuinely continuous pipelines the calendarTokensPerMinute view becomes the more honest rate to size against.

entries.title: Agent hours per day
entries.what: Hours per day the workflows are actively processing. Batch style automation might run a nightly four hour window; monitoring agents run all 24.
entries.why: Agent hours define whether this workload is bursty or a flat continuous load, and that shape decides which serving strategy is efficient. Flat continuous load is exactly where owned capacity starts earning its keep.
entries.math: Multiplies inside agentsMonthlyTokens with workflows and agDays. It also widens the active window in weightedTokensPerMinute, lowering the peak rate implied by the same monthly token total.

entries.title: Developers
entries.what: How many developers actively use the coding assistant, not how many hold licenses. The gap between the two is routinely a factor of two.
entries.why: Coding spend scales with hands on keyboards, and padding this number with the full engineering org is the most common sizing error in developer tooling. Count the people who will genuinely use it every week.
entries.math: A direct multiplier in codingMonthlyTokens: tokens per developer hour times developers times codDays times codHours. A positive count satisfies the checkDoNotSize usage gate for coding only scenarios.

entries.title: Coding days per month
entries.what: Days per month those developers actually code with the assistant. Around 21 working days is typical; heavy meeting cultures and split roles push it lower.
entries.why: At roughly 91 thousand tokens per developer hour, the gap between calendar days and real coding days is real money. This field anchors the estimate to engineering reality rather than the calendar.
entries.math: Multiplies inside codingMonthlyTokens and adds its window to the weightedTokensPerMinute denominator. Cutting it from 30 to 21 cuts the coding line item by 30 percent, straight through to totalMonthlyTokens.

entries.title: Coding hours per day
entries.what: Hours per day of active assisted coding, which is smaller than hours at work. Most engineers sustain three to six focused coding hours; the rest is meetings, review, and reading.
entries.why: Billing follows usage and usage follows focus time. Sizing to the eight hour workday systematically overstates assistant demand and erodes trust in the whole estimate when telemetry arrives.
entries.math: The final multiplier in codingMonthlyTokens. Like every quick schedule field it also stretches the active window used by weightedTokensPerMinute, trading peak intensity for duration in the demand rate.

entries.title: Agentic coding developers
entries.what: Developers running autonomous coding agents, tools that plan, edit, execute tests, and iterate on their own. Usually a smaller and more senior subset than the assistant user base.
entries.why: One agentic developer can drive several concurrent agent sessions, each burning tokens through full tool loops, so a small count here can outweigh a large assistant population. Track this group separately or the total will mislead.
entries.math: A direct multiplier in agenticCodingMonthlyTokens at the higher agentic heuristic rate. A positive count satisfies the checkDoNotSize usage gate when agentic coding is the only workload enabled.

entries.title: Agentic coding days per month
entries.what: Days per month the agentic coding practice actually runs. Teams experimenting touch it a few days; teams that have operationalized it approach the full working month.
entries.why: Agentic adoption is bimodal, occasional experiment or daily habit, and monthly cost differs by an order of magnitude between the two. This field is where that difference gets stated instead of assumed.
entries.math: Multiplies inside agenticCodingMonthlyTokens with acDevelopers and acHours, then flows into totalMonthlyTokens and the worker rate quick pricing in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Agentic coding hours per day
entries.what: Hours per day agents are actively working per developer. This can exceed a human coding day, because agents keep running while their operator reviews, plans, or sleeps.
entries.why: The instinct to cap this at human hours undercounts the whole point of agentic tooling: unattended runtime. Sizing it honestly is how overnight test and fix loops make it into the budget instead of arriving as a surprise.
entries.math: The final multiplier in agenticCodingMonthlyTokens at roughly 104 thousand tokens per hour. Its window also joins weightedTokensPerMinute, and large values push the workload toward the steady shape where the ceilingMonthly ownership math starts to bind.

entries.title: Users
entries.what: The total number of people licensed or expected to use the agent workload. This is headcount of the whole population, not how many are active at once. If 500 employees will have access, this is 500.
entries.why: Every sizing exercise starts with people, because Customers usually know headcount even when they know nothing else about their future usage. Everything after this field is a habit multiplier applied to this population.
entries.math: First factor in monthlyRuns = users times runsPerUserPerDay times activeDaysPerMonth times adoptionFactor times seasonalityMultiplier. Every downstream token, dollar, and GPU count multiplies from monthlyRuns, so doubling users doubles the entire demand side of the model.

entries.title: Concurrent users
entries.what: How many users are actively mid run at the same moment during the busy window. This is distinct from total users: 500 licensed people might show up as 25 simultaneous during a normal hour. Telephone engineers have measured exactly this distinction for a century.
entries.why: Peak infrastructure is sized to simultaneity, not headcount. Two Customers with identical monthly volume need very different hardware if one spreads work across the day and the other piles it into a morning rush.
entries.math: Feeds peakConcurrentRuns = concurrentUsers times peakConcurrencyFactor, which drives requiredPeakOutputTps and requiredRequestsPerSecond and through them gpusRequiredByThroughput. It never touches monthlyRuns, so it moves the machine count, not the monthly bill.

entries.title: Runs per user per day
entries.what: How many complete agent runs one active user triggers in a working day. A run is a whole task from request to final answer, not a single model call. One run may contain dozens of model calls depending on topology.
entries.why: This is the habit variable. It separates an occasional helper at 2 runs per day from an embedded workflow tool at 40, and Customers consistently underestimate it once adoption sticks and the agent becomes reflexive.
entries.math: Second factor in monthlyRuns. Each run then gets multiplied by totalCallsPerRun and the token anatomy, so a small bump here fans out through topology, retries, and per call token counts simultaneously.

entries.title: Active days per month
entries.what: How many days per month the workload actually runs. An office workload is around 22 working days. A background pipeline or Customer facing service can claim close to 30.
entries.why: It converts a daily habit into a monthly volume without pretending weekends exist for office users. Getting this wrong by claiming 30 days for a 22 day workload silently inflates the bill by more than a third.
entries.math: Third factor in monthlyRuns, and together with activeHoursPerDay it defines activeSecondsPerMonth inside requiredSteadyOutputTps. Raising it grows monthly tokens but lowers the required steady rate for the same total, because the work spreads across more time.

entries.title: Active hours per day
entries.what: Hours per active day the workload is actually busy. Office assistants live in an 8 hour window. Unattended pipelines can genuinely claim 24.
entries.why: Demand concentrated into fewer hours needs faster hardware even when the monthly total is identical. This field defines the active window that private inference sizing must survive, which is why bursty and steady workloads size so differently.
entries.math: Sets activeSecondsPerMonth in requiredSteadyOutputTps = agentMonthlyOutputTokens divided by activeSecondsPerMonth, and contributes active minutes to weightedTokensPerMinute. It does not change monthlyRuns; it changes how hard those runs hit per second.

entries.title: Adoption percent
entries.what: The share of licensed users who genuinely use the agent in a given month. Real deployments rarely see 100 percent. Pilots commonly run 30 to 60, and mature rollouts climb from there.
entries.why: Sizing to license counts instead of actual adopters is the classic overbuy. Adoption also grows over time, so this single field is where the pilot scenario and the mature scenario differ most, and it deserves a run at both settings.
entries.math: Divided by 100 into adoptionFactor inside monthlyRuns. At 50 percent it halves runs, tokens, provider cost, and therefore ceilingMonthly and ceilingCapex in one move, so it directly moves the hardware verdict.

entries.title: Peak concurrency factor
entries.what: A multiplier that turns typical simultaneous usage into worst window simultaneous usage. If a 9am rush triples normal concurrency, this factor is 3. A perfectly flat workload is 1.
entries.why: Users experience the peak, not the average. Hardware sized to the average queues badly at exactly the moment everyone is watching, and that is how private inference deployments get a bad reputation on day one.
entries.math: Multiplies concurrentUsers into peakConcurrentRuns, which feeds requiredPeakOutputTps and requiredRequestsPerSecond, and through them gpusRequiredByThroughput. It has zero effect on monthly cost; it only moves the machine count gate.

entries.title: Seasonality multiplier
entries.what: A scaling factor for calendar effects on monthly volume. Retail Customer support in December might run 1.5. A quiet August might run 0.8. A workload with no seasonal pattern stays at 1.
entries.why: A single average month misrepresents any business with cyclical demand. Sizing decisions should be tested against the month you will actually be judged on, not the statistical middle of the year.
entries.math: Final factor of monthlyRuns, so it scales the entire demand chain including agentMonthlyInputTokens, provider cost, and ceilingMonthly. Run the scenario at both the quiet and busy settings before committing to hardware.

entries.title: Growth percent
entries.what: Expected growth in workload volume over the planning horizon, expressed as a percent. It captures trajectory: a pilot at 100 users heading toward 300 is a fundamentally different purchase than a flat 100.
entries.why: Providers scale with a credit card while owned hardware is bought at a point in time. Whether the hardware ceiling verdict survives twelve months of growth is often the entire argument between hybrid and owned routes.
entries.math: A planning assumption rather than a live formula factor today: none of the formula cards consume it directly. Apply it as a stress test by rerunning the scenario with users or runsPerUserPerDay scaled up by this percent, then check whether recommendedGpuCount and the ceilingCapex verdict flip.

entries.title: Topology
entries.what: The shape of the agent system: a single assistant, a planner with workers, an added judge, a swarm, a supervisor with specialists, a tool workflow, a background batch chain, or a human in the loop chain. Selecting a shape loads a preset of per run call counts for each role.
entries.why: Topology is the invisible cost multiplier. The same user request costs 2 model calls as a single assistant and 15 or more as a swarm, and Customers compare model prices while the topology quietly moves the bill by an order of magnitude.
entries.math: The preset patches plannerCalls, workerAgents, judgeCalls and the other role counts, which sum into baseCallsPerRun and then totalCallsPerRun. Everything downstream of monthlyRuns is multiplied by this shape, which is why the topology section exists at all.

entries.title: Planner calls per run
entries.what: Model calls per run that do planning: reading the request, decomposing it into steps, and assigning work to other roles. Structured topologies typically use 1; a single assistant uses 0.
entries.why: Planners carry the largest prompts because they must see the whole task to plan it, and they usually run the premium model. Each planner call is often the single most expensive call in the run.
entries.math: Adds directly into baseCallsPerRun and pairs with the roles.planner token counts inside effectiveRolePlan. Replans multiply this specific count: replanCallsPerRun = plannerCalls times the replan rate, attributed to the planner only.

entries.title: Router calls per run
entries.what: Calls per run that classify and dispatch: deciding which worker, tool, or model tier should handle a step. Router prompts are small and their outputs are tiny by design.
entries.why: Routers exist so expensive models only see work that deserves them. They are cheap per call but sit on the critical path of every step, so their count still lands in the run total.
entries.math: Counts into baseCallsPerRun; token weight comes from the roles.router fields inside effectiveRolePlan. The router anatomy fields only appear once this count is above zero.

entries.title: Worker agents per run
entries.what: How many distinct worker agents one run fans out to. Workers do the actual task work: drafting, extracting, coding, analyzing. A single assistant is 1 worker; a swarm might run 6 or more in parallel.
entries.why: Fan out is where swarms explode financially. Six workers each making three calls is 18 calls before any judge sees anything, and each of those calls carries context, tool results, and retrieved documents.
entries.math: Multiplied by workerCallsPerAgent inside baseCallsPerRun. In effectiveRolePlan the worker role also absorbs the run level extras, meaning tool result tokens, schema overhead, memory reads, and RAG context are all billed as worker input. This is usually the biggest single lever in totalCallsPerRun.

entries.title: Calls per worker agent
entries.what: How many model calls each worker makes to finish its slice of the task: think, act, and revise loops. Single shot extraction is 1. Iterative work commonly runs 3 to 5.
entries.why: Iteration depth per worker is a silent multiplier that demos hide. Production loops run longer than staged demos because real inputs are messier, and this field is where that reality gets priced.
entries.math: Partner of workerAgents inside baseCallsPerRun. When carry is enabled, every worker call also inherits contextSnowballTokensPerCall as extra input, so deep loops pay twice: more calls and fatter calls.

entries.title: Judge calls per run
entries.what: Calls per run that evaluate outputs: scoring, accepting, or rejecting what workers produced. Often 1 at the end of a run, sometimes 1 per major step in stricter systems.
entries.why: Judges buy quality and safety at the price of extra calls on what is usually a premium model. Regulated Customers often cannot ship without a grading gate, so this cost is frequently nonnegotiable.
entries.math: Adds into baseCallsPerRun and takes the roles.judge token weights in effectiveRolePlan. With carry enabled the judge inherits contextSnowballTokensPerCall per call just like workers, because it must read the carried work to grade it.

entries.title: Critic calls per run
entries.what: Calls that critique and request revisions rather than simply pass or fail. A critic feeds notes back into workers for another pass; a judge gates the final output.
entries.why: Critique loops raise quality on hard tasks, but each round of critique triggers another round of worker calls, so the effective cost multiplier is larger than the critic count alone suggests.
entries.math: Counts into baseCallsPerRun with the roles.critic token weights in effectiveRolePlan. The critic anatomy fields only surface when this count is above zero, and the induced rework shows up through workerCallsPerAgent.

entries.title: Summarizer calls per run
entries.what: Calls that compress: condensing worker output, tool results, or long context into short summaries for the next step or the final answer.
entries.why: Summarizers are the cheapest defense against the context snowball. A small model spending a few hundred tokens once can save thousands of carried tokens on every later call.
entries.math: Adds into baseCallsPerRun with the roles.summarizer weights. In effectiveRolePlan, memory write summaries are billed as output at the summarizer rate when this count is above zero; otherwise the worker rate absorbs them.

entries.title: Memory read calls per run
entries.what: Calls per run that fetch long term memory: prior conversations, Customer preferences, and learned facts pulled in before or during the work.
entries.why: Memory is what makes agents feel continuous across sessions, and every read injects stored tokens back into the prompt as fresh input cost. Continuity is a feature with a per run price.
entries.math: Counts into baseCallsPerRun. The token payload comes from memoryReadTokensPerRun in the memory section, which effectiveRolePlan bills as worker input scaled by the retryScale factor.

entries.title: Memory write calls per run
entries.what: Calls per run that store new memory: summarizing what happened and writing it down for later recall.
entries.why: Writes are output tokens, the expensive direction, and they compound. Everything written today becomes read cost on every future run that recalls it, so write discipline matters twice.
entries.math: Counts into baseCallsPerRun. The token weight rides in memoryWriteTokensPerRun and lands as output on the summarizer role when one exists, otherwise on the worker, inside effectiveRolePlan.

entries.title: RAG calls per run
entries.what: How many retrieval queries one run issues against the document index. Each query pulls a set of chunks out of the vector store and into the prompt.
entries.why: Retrieved context is usually the biggest single input line item in a knowledge grounded agent, and multiplying retrievals per run multiplies it directly. One retrieval versus four is a real architectural decision.
entries.math: Counts into baseCallsPerRun and multiplies retrievedContextTokens inside the worker input extras of effectiveRolePlan. It also drives monthlyEmbeddingTokens at about 40 query tokens each, and peakRagQps inside ragTrafficMbps.

entries.title: Tool planning calls per run
entries.what: Calls spent deciding which tool to invoke and with what arguments before the tool actually runs. Tool using workflows typically spend 1 or 2 of these per run.
entries.why: Tool choice calls carry the entire exposed tool catalog as prompt overhead, so a fat catalog taxes every one of these calls before any real work happens. Catalog size and this count are partners in cost.
entries.math: Adds into baseCallsPerRun. In effectiveRolePlan, toolSchemaOverheadPerCall is multiplied by toolPlanningCalls plus toolResultSummaryCalls and billed as worker input, so this count directly gears the schema overhead line.

entries.title: Tool result summary calls per run
entries.what: Calls that condense raw tool output into a short digest before it enters the working context. The raw output of an API or a file read is often enormous compared to what the agent actually needs.
entries.why: Summarizing tool output at the boundary is frequently the single best cost control in tool heavy agents, because raw results otherwise ride along in context for the rest of the run.
entries.math: Adds into baseCallsPerRun and joins toolPlanningCalls in the schema overhead multiplier inside effectiveRolePlan. The size of the digests themselves is set in the tools section by toolResultSummaryTokens, which feeds toolResultTokensPerRun.

entries.title: Retry rate percent
entries.what: The percent of model calls that fail and rerun: timeouts, malformed output, validation failures. 10 percent means one call in ten executes twice on average.
entries.why: Retries are silent cost. They appear on the invoice but never in the architecture diagram, and production agent systems commonly run 5 to 15 percent even when healthy.
entries.math: retryCallsPerRun = baseCallsPerRun times the retry rate, folded into totalCallsPerRun. Inside effectiveRolePlan the retryScale factor also inflates the run level tool, memory, and RAG tokens, because failed calls consumed those resources too.

entries.title: Replan rate percent
entries.what: The percent of runs where the planner must discard its plan and produce a new one after work has already started, usually because a step failed or the task turned out different than it looked.
entries.why: Replans repeat the priciest role in the system. A rising replan rate signals ambiguous tasks or flaky tools, and it multiplies planner spend specifically rather than spreading across all roles.
entries.math: replanCallsPerRun = plannerCalls times the replan rate, added into totalCallsPerRun. In effectiveRolePlan those calls are attributed as extra planner calls only, per the spec attribution rule, so they carry full planner token weights.

entries.title: Escalation to premium model percent
entries.what: The percent of runs escalated from the routine model tier to a premium model because the cheap tier could not handle them. Think of it as triage: most cases stay downstairs, hard cases go up.
entries.why: Escalation is the honest middle path of agent economics: run the fleet on efficient models and pay for top intelligence only when the work demands it. A nonzero value here is the signature of a hybrid architecture.
entries.math: Today this field drives route scoring rather than token math: any value above zero adds points to the hybrid route in the visible scoring panel. Model the cost effect itself in the routing section by assigning provider tiers per role, which roleRoutedCost then prices.

entries.title: Max loop depth
entries.what: The hard cap on how many times an agent may loop on the same step before the system forces an exit. It is a guardrail you configure, not a prediction of typical behavior.
entries.why: Unbounded loops are the pathological cost case: an agent that cannot satisfy its own judge will happily burn tokens forever. Every production deployment needs this circuit breaker, and its value defines your worst case run cost.
entries.math: A control assumption rather than a formula input: no card consumes it directly. It bounds the worst case behind the averages in baseCallsPerRun, so sanity check that workerCallsPerAgent and retry assumptions sit well below what this cap allows, or the averages are optimistic.

entries.title: Planner input tokens per call
entries.what: Prompt tokens one planner call consumes: system instructions, the user request, task state, and whatever context is needed to decompose the work. Planners read the most of any role, commonly thousands to tens of thousands of tokens per call.
entries.why: Input per call times calls is the anatomy of the bill, and the planner prompt is usually the fattest in the run because it must see everything to plan anything. It also typically runs on the premium tier, so these tokens are priced at the top rate.
entries.math: Multiplied by planner calls, including replanCallsPerRun, inside effectiveRolePlan. The result sums into inputTokensPerRun and then agentMonthlyInputTokens = monthlyRuns times inputTokensPerRun, which roleRoutedCost prices per tier.

entries.title: Planner output tokens per call
entries.what: Completion tokens one planner call emits: the plan itself, step lists, and work assignments. Usually far smaller than the planner input, often a few hundred to a couple thousand tokens.
entries.why: Output tokens price at roughly five times input at every shipped provider rate, so even modest plans cost real money at volume. Replans emit whole new plans, doubling this line on affected runs.
entries.math: Multiplied by planner calls plus replanCallsPerRun in effectiveRolePlan, summing into outputTokensPerRun and agentMonthlyOutputTokens. Output volume also drives requiredSteadyOutputTps, so this field touches hardware sizing too.

entries.title: Worker input tokens per call
entries.what: Prompt tokens one worker call reads: its assignment from the planner, the relevant slice of context, and the material it must work on.
entries.why: Workers make the most calls in nearly every topology, so this number gears the largest share of input spend. Trimming worker prompts is usually worth more than trimming any other role.
entries.math: In effectiveRolePlan the worker line is calls times this value plus snowball tokens per call, plus all run level extras billed at the worker rate: tool results, schema overhead, memory reads, and RAG context. It dominates inputTokensPerRun in worker heavy shapes.

entries.title: Worker output tokens per call
entries.what: Completion tokens one worker emits per call: the draft, the extraction, the code, the analysis. This is the actual product of the system.
entries.why: The expensive token direction multiplied by the most numerous role makes this typically the single largest output line on the bill. Verbose workers also inflate judge and critic input downstream, since those roles must read what workers wrote.
entries.math: Calls times this value in effectiveRolePlan, summing into outputTokensPerRun, then agentMonthlyOutputTokens, which feeds both roleRoutedCost and the requiredPeakOutputTps throughput gate.

entries.title: Judge input tokens per call
entries.what: Prompt tokens a judge call reads: the grading rubric plus the material being graded. An honest judge must read the work in full, so this scales with worker output size.
entries.why: Grading cost is a function of what workers produce, which means cutting worker verbosity pays twice: once on worker output and again on judge input. Customers with quality gates should watch this coupling.
entries.math: Calls times this value in effectiveRolePlan, plus contextSnowballTokensPerCall per call when carry is enabled, since judges read carried context too. Sums into inputTokensPerRun.

entries.title: Judge output tokens per call
entries.what: Completion tokens a judge emits: scores, verdicts, and brief reasoning. Properly small, usually tens to a few hundred tokens.
entries.why: A judge writing essays is a critic wearing the wrong badge. Keeping verdicts short keeps the quality gate affordable, especially since judges typically run on the premium tier.
entries.math: Calls times this value into outputTokensPerRun through effectiveRolePlan. The absolute number is small, but roleRoutedCost prices it at whatever tier the judge is routed to, which is usually the most expensive one.

entries.title: Summarizer input tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens a summarizer reads per call: the long material being compressed, whether that is worker output, tool results, or accumulated context.
entries.why: The summarizer input is the price of compression, paid once so that every downstream step can pay less repeatedly. It looks expensive in isolation and cheap in context.
entries.math: Calls times this value into inputTokensPerRun via effectiveRolePlan; these fields only appear when summarizerCalls is above zero. The payoff side shows up in contextSnowballTokensPerCall when summarizeCarry applies the compression ratio.

entries.title: Summarizer output tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens the summary itself contains. This is the compression target, often 10 to 20 percent of the input size for a good summarizer.
entries.why: Every later consumer of the summary inherits this size, so it sets the ongoing cost of the compressed context. Too small and detail is lost; too large and the compression bought nothing.
entries.math: Calls times this value into outputTokensPerRun in effectiveRolePlan. When memory writes exist, memoryWriteTokensPerRun also lands here as summarizer output, since summaries are how memory gets written.

entries.title: Router input tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens a router call reads: a short classification prompt plus the item being routed. Small by design, often just a few hundred tokens.
entries.why: Routing must stay cheap or it defeats its own purpose. If the router prompt rivals a worker prompt in size, the dispatch layer is mispriced and the architecture needs another look.
entries.math: Calls times this value into inputTokensPerRun via effectiveRolePlan; visible only when routerCalls is above zero. Routers typically map to the mini tier in roleRoutedCost, keeping this line tiny.

entries.title: Router output tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens a router emits: a label, a route name, perhaps a small set of arguments. Should be tiny, often under 50 tokens.
entries.why: Anything long coming out of a router is a design smell. The entire point of the role is a fast cheap decision, and the output size is the proof of discipline.
entries.math: Calls times this value into outputTokensPerRun through effectiveRolePlan. At healthy sizes this line rounds to noise in agentMonthlyOutputTokens, which is exactly the goal.

entries.title: Critic input tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens a critic reads per call: the work under review plus the critique instructions. Like judges, critics must read everything they evaluate.
entries.why: Worker verbosity flows straight into critic cost, and critique heavy topologies pay this on every revision cycle. It is the reading half of the quality loop.
entries.math: Calls times this value into inputTokensPerRun in effectiveRolePlan; the critic anatomy fields only appear when criticCalls is above zero.

entries.title: Critic output tokens per call
entries.what: Tokens of critique the critic writes: specific, actionable revision notes for the workers to act on.
entries.why: Critiques are consumed by workers as fresh input on the next pass, so a verbose critic taxes two roles at once: its own output line and the worker input line that rereads it.
entries.math: Calls times this value into outputTokensPerRun in effectiveRolePlan. The induced rework cost shows up separately through workerCallsPerAgent and the retry assumptions, not in this line.

entries.title: Cached input percent
entries.what: The percent of input tokens served from the provider prompt cache: repeated system prompts, tool schemas, and shared context the provider recognizes and reprices instead of reprocessing. Agent workloads with stable scaffolding legitimately reach high rates.
entries.why: Cached input commonly bills at about 10 percent of the normal input price, making this one of the largest honest levers on the bill. Agents repeat their own scaffolding constantly, which is exactly what caches reward.
entries.math: Splits agentMonthlyInputTokens into cachedInputTokensMonthly and uncachedInputTokensMonthly. roleRoutedCost prices the cached share at the cached rate, and the promptCachingSavings card shows the delta against running with no cache at all.

entries.title: Batch eligible percent
entries.what: The percent of the workload that can wait: work with no human sitting in front of it, eligible for provider batch endpoints that return within hours at roughly half price. Pipelines, backfills, and report generation usually qualify.
entries.why: Interactive and background work should never be priced the same. Identifying the batchable share is close to free money, but it is only honest for work that genuinely tolerates delayed completion.
entries.math: roleRoutedCost applies the provider batchDiscountMultiplier to this share of the cost and reports it as batchSavings in the cost breakdown. The optimization levers panel suggests 80 percent for background heavy scenarios.

entries.title: Carry forward previous step context
entries.what: A toggle stating whether later steps carry earlier steps' context forward inside their prompts. Most multi step agents do this by default unless deliberately engineered not to, because it is the easiest way to keep steps coherent.
entries.why: Carrying context is how agents remember what they just did, and it is also how input cost quietly multiplies: step five pays to reread steps one through four. This toggle makes that architectural fact visible instead of buried.
entries.math: Gates contextSnowballTokensPerCall entirely. When on, effectiveRolePlan adds the snowball tokens to the input of every worker and judge call; when off, the snowball is zero and the fields below fold away.

entries.title: Carry forward percent
entries.what: The share of each prior step's tokens actually included when history is carried forward. 100 means full replay of everything; 30 means only the important third survives the cut.
entries.why: This measures context discipline. Naive implementations replay everything; engineered ones select what matters, and the difference compounds across every carried step on every call.
entries.math: A direct multiplier inside contextSnowballTokensPerCall = carriedSteps times the sum of prior step input and output tokens, times this share, times the compression ratio. Halving it halves the snowball.

entries.title: Average prior step input tokens
entries.what: The average input size of one earlier step in the chain, used to estimate how much history exists to be carried forward.
entries.why: The snowball is built from what earlier steps consumed and produced, so realistic step sizes keep the whole estimate honest. Guessing small here hides the dominant input cost in deep chains.
entries.math: Summed with priorStepOutputTokens inside contextSnowballTokensPerCall, then scaled by carriedSteps, the carry share, and the compression ratio before landing on worker and judge calls in effectiveRolePlan.

entries.title: Average prior step output tokens
entries.what: The average output size of one earlier step: what it actually wrote, which later steps may need to see to continue the work.
entries.why: Outputs are usually the part of history worth carrying, since they hold the decisions, drafts, and results, so this value often dominates the carried payload.
entries.math: Added to priorStepInputTokens inside contextSnowballTokensPerCall before the carry share and compression multipliers apply. Together they define the token mass of one step of history.

entries.title: Number of carried steps
entries.what: How many previous steps a typical call drags along in its prompt. Five carried steps means every late call rereads five steps worth of history before doing anything new.
entries.why: This is the depth of the snowball. Cost grows linearly with this count on each call, then multiplies across all worker and judge calls in the run, which is how deep chains surprise people.
entries.math: The leading multiplier of contextSnowballTokensPerCall. effectiveRolePlan then charges that amount per call on workers and judges, inflating inputTokensPerRun and everything priced from it.

entries.title: Summarize previous steps before carry
entries.what: A toggle for compressing carried history through a summarizer before it rides along, instead of replaying the raw text of earlier steps.
entries.why: Summarize once, save on every subsequent call. This is the standard cure when the snowball dominates input spend, and it is the reason the summarizer role exists in most mature agent designs.
entries.math: When on, contextSnowballTokensPerCall multiplies by summaryCompressionRatio; when off, the ratio is 1 and the full carried weight applies. Pair it with real summarizerCalls so the compression work itself is also counted.

entries.title: Summary compression ratio
entries.what: The size of a summary relative to its source material. 0.15 means the summary is 15 percent of the original token count. Lower is more aggressive compression.
entries.why: This sets how much the summarization defense actually buys. Aggressive ratios save more tokens but risk dropping detail that later steps need, and that loss reappears as retries and replans elsewhere in the model.
entries.math: The final multiplier in contextSnowballTokensPerCall when summarizeCarry is on. Moving from 1.0 to 0.15 cuts the snowball by 85 percent across every affected worker and judge call, directly shrinking inputTokensPerRun.

entries.title: RAG enabled
entries.what: A master switch declaring whether this system uses retrieval augmented generation: searching a document index and pasting the best matches into the prompt before the model answers. It is how a model gets grounded in the Customer's own documents instead of relying on what it memorized in training.
entries.why: RAG changes the cost profile of every single query, because retrieved text rides along as input tokens on each call. It exists as a toggle so that systems with no retrieval keep a clean model, and so the calculator can show exactly what turning retrieval on adds to the bill.
entries.math: Gates the entire RAG chain: chunksPerDocument, retrievedContextTokens, vectorRecords, and monthlyEmbeddingTokens only compute when it is on. Inside effectiveRolePlan, retrievedContextTokens times ragCallsPerRun is added to worker input tokens, flowing through inputTokensPerRun into agentMonthlyInputTokens and roleRoutedCost. It also unlocks the embedding fee line and ragTrafficMbps in network sizing.

related.label: The original RAG paper (Lewis et al., 2020)

entries.title: Vector store provider
entries.what: The product that stores document embeddings and answers similarity searches, for example pgvector, Milvus, Weaviate, Pinecone, or a cloud vendor's native store. Embeddings are numeric fingerprints of text; the vector store is the database that finds the closest fingerprints to a query.
entries.why: The choice decides where the Customer's embeddings physically live, which collides directly with the policy gate: if embeddings cannot leave the environment, a SaaS vector store is off the table. Naming it also makes the sizing report read as a concrete architecture instead of an abstract one.
entries.math: This field carries no arithmetic; it is recorded for report language and route reasoning, not fed into a formula. The store it names is sized next door: vectorRecords = documentsIndexed times chunksPerDocument, and vectorDbLegacyGB estimates its ongoing growth.

entries.title: Documents indexed
entries.what: The total count of documents sitting in the retrieval corpus: every PDF, wiki page, contract, and manual the system can search. This is the size of the library, not the rate anyone reads from it.
entries.why: Corpus size is the number Customers always know off the top of their head, so it anchors the RAG conversation. The teaching point is counterintuitive: a bigger library does not raise per query prompt cost, because each query still retrieves a fixed number of chunks; what it raises is index size and initial embedding work.
entries.math: Feeds vectorRecords = documentsIndexed times chunksPerDocument, which sizes the vector database and its memory footprint. It does not appear in retrievedContextTokens or inputTokensPerRun, which is exactly the lesson: storage scales with the corpus, token spend scales with retrieval depth and query volume.

entries.title: Average pages per document
entries.what: How long a typical document in the corpus runs, in pages. A support macro might be one page; a master services agreement might be sixty. This is an average across the whole library, so a few giants and many small files still produce a usable number.
entries.why: Nobody counts tokens in their document library, but everyone can estimate pages. This field, paired with tokens per page, converts a human answer into the token measure every downstream formula needs.
entries.math: Multiplied by tokensPerPage to form documentTokens, the numerator of chunksPerDocument = ceil(documentTokens / (chunkSize minus chunkOverlap)). It also scales the churn term of monthlyEmbeddingTokens, where new and changed documents are converted to tokens per month. Raising it grows the vector index and the embedding bill, not the per query prompt.

entries.title: Average tokens per page
entries.what: The conversion rate between a page of text and model tokens. A token is the unit models actually read and bill by, roughly three quarters of an English word; a dense page of prose typically lands between 400 and 600 tokens.
entries.why: Every formula downstream runs on tokens, and this is the bridge from the page counts humans can supply. It also varies by content: tables, code, and non English text tokenize heavier, so a Customer with dense technical docs should push this number up.
entries.math: Combines with avgDocPages to produce documentTokens, which drives chunksPerDocument and therefore vectorRecords. It also scales monthlyEmbeddingTokens through the changed document term, so it moves the embedding fee inside roleRoutedCost whenever an embedding price is set.

entries.title: Chunk size (tokens)
entries.what: Documents are too big to embed whole, so they are sliced into chunks, and this is the size of each slice in tokens. The chunk is the atomic unit of retrieval: it is what gets embedded, what gets stored as a vector record, and what comes back when a query matches.
entries.why: Chunk size is the core retrieval tradeoff. Small chunks give precise matches but multiply record counts and can strand context across boundaries; large chunks keep context together but drag more irrelevant text into every prompt and make each retrieval hit more expensive.
entries.math: Sets the denominator of chunksPerDocument = ceil(documentTokens / (chunkSize minus chunkOverlap)), so smaller chunks mean more vector records via vectorRecords. In practice it also anchors avgTokensPerChunk, which multiplies straight into retrievedContextTokens and from there into worker input cost in roleRoutedCost.

related.label: The original RAG paper (Lewis et al., 2020)

entries.title: Chunk overlap (tokens)
entries.what: The number of tokens repeated between adjacent chunks when a document is sliced. Overlap exists so a sentence that straddles a chunk boundary appears whole in at least one chunk instead of being cut mid thought.
entries.why: Without overlap, the exact passage that answers a question can be split across two chunks and match neither. The price of that insurance is duplication: every token of overlap is embedded and stored more than once, inflating the index.
entries.math: Subtracts from the effective stride in chunksPerDocument = ceil(documentTokens / (chunkSize minus chunkOverlap)), so more overlap means more chunks, more vectorRecords, and more embedding work. The calculator refuses to compute when overlap reaches or exceeds chunkSize and raises a critical warning instead of producing garbage.

entries.title: Chunks retrieved per query
entries.what: How many of the best matching chunks each retrieval query pulls back and injects into the prompt, often called top k. Retrieval ranks every chunk in the store by similarity to the query and hands the model the top few.
entries.why: This is the single most direct RAG cost lever, because it multiplies into every query the system ever runs. Research on long contexts shows models attend poorly to material buried mid prompt, so piling on chunks past a certain point costs real money for little quality.
entries.math: Multiplies avgTokensPerChunk to form retrievedContextTokens, which effectiveRolePlan adds to worker input for every RAG call per run, flowing into inputTokensPerRun, agentMonthlyInputTokens, and roleRoutedCost. The optimization levers panel prices the halve retrieved chunks lever from exactly this field.

related.label: Lost in the Middle: how models use long contexts

entries.title: Average tokens per retrieved chunk
entries.what: The actual size of a chunk as it arrives in the prompt, in tokens. It usually tracks chunkSize, but can differ when the store returns trimmed passages, expanded windows around a hit, or metadata alongside the text.
entries.why: It is kept separate from chunkSize because indexing size and retrieval payload size are different facts, and conflating them hides cost. What lands in the prompt is what gets billed, so this field is the honest measure of retrieval weight.
entries.math: The second factor in retrievedContextTokens = chunksRetrievedPerQuery times avgTokensPerChunk. That product, scaled by ragCallsPerRun and the retry scale inside effectiveRolePlan, is billed as worker input in roleRoutedCost, which is why retrieved context is usually the biggest single input line item.

entries.title: Reranker enabled
entries.what: A reranker is a second stage model that takes the vector search candidates and reorders them by true relevance before the top few go to the prompt. Vector similarity is fast but shallow; a reranker reads query and passage together and judges the pair properly.
entries.why: Rerankers let you retrieve fewer chunks at equal or better answer quality, which is the polite way to cut the biggest input line item. Recording the choice also tells the report whether retrieval quality has a safety net or is riding on raw similarity scores alone.
entries.math: No direct arithmetic today; the toggle is captured for report and architecture context rather than a formula. Its economic effect shows up indirectly: with a reranker on you can lower chunksRetrievedPerQuery, which shrinks retrievedContextTokens and everything downstream of it in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Daily new documents
entries.what: How many brand new documents enter the corpus each day: new tickets, new contracts, new wiki pages. Each one must be chunked and embedded before it is findable, so this is the arrival rate of indexing work.
entries.why: A corpus is almost never static, and churn is the part of RAG cost that quietly persists forever. Indexing the library once is a project; keeping it current is an operating expense, and this field makes that expense visible.
entries.math: Summed with dailyChangedDocuments, multiplied by 30 and by tokens per document, it forms the monthlyChangedDocTokens term of monthlyEmbeddingTokens. That total, priced by embeddingPricePerMillion, becomes the embedding fee added to the roleRoutedCost monthly total.

entries.title: Daily changed documents
entries.what: How many existing documents are edited each day and must be re chunked and re embedded. An edited document's old vectors are stale; the pipeline has to reprocess it as if it were new.
entries.why: Change rate is the churn most teams forget to count, and in living knowledge bases it often exceeds the new document rate. A policy wiki edited hourly costs embedding money every single day even if not one new page is ever added.
entries.math: Treated identically to new documents in the math: (dailyNewDocuments plus dailyChangedDocuments) times 30 times tokens per document forms monthlyChangedDocTokens inside monthlyEmbeddingTokens. From there it flows into the embedding fee in roleRoutedCost when a price per million is set.

entries.title: Embedding price per million tokens (USD)
entries.what: What the chosen embedding model charges per million tokens processed. Embedding models are small and cheap compared with chat models, typically pennies to a few dimes per million tokens, but they touch every token of every indexed and re indexed document plus every query.
entries.why: The calculator deliberately makes this user supplied instead of shipping a default, because embedding prices vary by model and change often, and a silently stale price corrupts trust in the whole sheet. Entering the Customer's real rate keeps the embedding line honest.
entries.math: Multiplies monthlyEmbeddingTokens divided by one million to produce the embeddingFee that roleRoutedCost adds to the monthly total. When left blank, embedding tokens are still counted and displayed, but an info warning states they are excluded from provider cost totals until a price exists.

entries.title: Tool use enabled
entries.what: Declares whether the agent can call external systems: search APIs, databases, CRMs, code runners. In tool use, the model is shown a catalog of tool descriptions, decides to invoke one, and the result is pasted back into the conversation for it to read.
entries.why: Tool use is what separates an agent from a chatbot, and it introduces two token costs people rarely anticipate: the catalog of tool schemas rides along as input on calls, and every tool result becomes input tokens the model must re read. The toggle keeps that whole tax off the books for systems that do not need it.
entries.math: Gates toolSchemaOverheadPerCall, toolResultTokensPerRun, and toolTrafficMbps. Inside effectiveRolePlan, tool result tokens plus schema overhead times the tool call count are added to worker input, retry scaled, and priced through roleRoutedCost. Two optimization levers, summarize results and shrink the catalog, only exist when this is on.

related.label: ReAct: reasoning and acting in language models

entries.title: Tools exposed
entries.what: How many tools are described to the model on each call. Every exposed tool contributes its schema, the structured description of its name, purpose, and parameters, to the prompt, whether or not that tool ever gets used in the run.
entries.why: Teams bolt on tools until the catalog is huge, and each addition silently taxes every call in the system. A model shown forty tools pays for forty schemas per call while typically using two, so curating the exposed set is one of the cheapest optimizations available.
entries.math: Multiplies avgToolSchemaTokens to form toolSchemaOverheadPerCall, which effectiveRolePlan charges as worker input scaled by the tool planning and summary call count. The formula card warns when overhead passes about 4,000 tokens per call, and the halve the exposed tool catalog lever in optimizationLevers prices the fix.

entries.title: Average schema tokens per tool
entries.what: The token weight of one tool's description: its name, purpose, parameter names, types, and usage notes, usually serialized as JSON schema. A terse tool runs under 100 tokens; a richly documented enterprise API can exceed 1,000.
entries.why: Schema verbosity is a real engineering choice with a real bill attached. Detailed schemas make the model call tools more accurately, but every token of description is repeated on every call forever, so this field forces the documentation versus overhead tradeoff into the open.
entries.math: The second factor in toolSchemaOverheadPerCall = toolsExposed times avgToolSchemaTokens. That overhead enters worker input inside effectiveRolePlan, retry scales with the calls that carry it, and lands in roleRoutedCost, so a 200 token trim across a large catalog compounds into visible monthly savings.

entries.title: Tool calls per run
entries.what: How many times a single agent run actually invokes a tool: three database lookups plus one web search is four tool calls. This counts executions, not the size of the catalog, which is toolsExposed.
entries.why: Each tool call produces a result the model must then read as input tokens, so chatty agents that poke at systems repeatedly pay twice: once in latency, once in re read cost. Knowing the per run count is what turns tool behavior into a sizable number.
entries.math: Multiplies tokensPerResult to form toolResultTokensPerRun, where tokensPerResult is either avgToolResultTokens or the summary size depending on toolResultSummarization. It also drives peak tool calls per second in toolTrafficMbps, connecting agent chattiness to network sizing.

related.label: ReAct: reasoning and acting in language models

entries.title: Average tool result tokens
entries.what: The token size of what a tool sends back: the API response, query result set, or page content that gets pasted into the conversation. Raw enterprise API responses are notoriously bloated; a single verbose JSON payload can run thousands of tokens.
entries.why: Tool results are the silent twin of schema overhead. The model must read every token of every result as input, so an agent wired to a talkative API can spend more on re reading results than on its own reasoning.
entries.math: Used as tokensPerResult in toolResultTokensPerRun = toolCallsPerRun times tokensPerResult whenever summarization is off. That product joins worker input in effectiveRolePlan, retry scaled, and is priced at the worker's rate in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Summarize tool results
entries.what: A design pattern where a cheap model pass compresses each raw tool result before it enters the main conversation, so the expensive model reads a distilled version instead of the full payload. The toggle records whether this system does that.
entries.why: It is one of the highest leverage cost controls in agent design: a 3,000 token API response compressed to a 300 token summary cuts that input line by ninety percent. The tradeoff is an extra summarization call and the risk of losing a detail the summary dropped.
entries.math: Switches tokensPerResult inside toolResultTokensPerRun from avgToolResultTokens to toolResultSummaryTokens. The summarize tool results before reinjecting lever in optimizationLevers re runs the whole engine with this toggled on and shows the exact monthly dollar effect.

entries.title: Summary tokens per result
entries.what: The target size of a tool result after compression, in tokens. This is the budget the summarization step aims for: small enough to be cheap, large enough to preserve the identifiers, values, and caveats the agent needs downstream.
