entries.why: The compression target is a real design decision, not an afterthought. Squeeze too hard and the agent re calls tools to recover lost details, which costs more than the tokens saved; too loose and the summarization step was theater.
entries.math: Replaces avgToolResultTokens as tokensPerResult in toolResultTokensPerRun when summarization is on, so the ratio between the two fields is the compression factor the whole savings case rests on. The product flows into worker input via effectiveRolePlan and is priced in roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Failed tool call rate percent
entries.what: The share of tool invocations that error out: timeouts, auth failures, malformed arguments, rate limits. Agents respond to failures by retrying the tool or rethinking the step, both of which burn additional model calls and tokens.
entries.why: Failure rate is an operational truth about the Customer's integrations that pure token math cannot see, and it predicts how far reality will drift above the estimate. A ten percent failure rate against a flaky internal API is a different system than a clean one, even at identical nominal volume.
entries.math: Captured for the report and risk framing; the token cost of failure is modeled through retryRatePercent, which drives retryCallsPerRun = baseCallsPerRun times the retry rate and the retryScale applied throughout effectiveRolePlan. Keep the two fields consistent: a high failure rate with a near zero retry rate understates the bill.

entries.title: Memory enabled
entries.what: Declares whether the agent has long term memory: a store of facts, preferences, and past outcomes it can read at the start of a run and write back to at the end. This persists across sessions, unlike the conversation context that vanishes when a run ends.
entries.why: Memory is what makes an agent feel continuous to its users, and it has a two sided token cost people miss: recall injects stored text as input on every run that reads, and writing means generating summaries, which are output tokens, the expensive kind.
entries.math: Gates memoryReadTokensPerRun and memoryWriteTokensPerRun. In effectiveRolePlan, read tokens join worker input and write tokens join output billed at the summarizer's rate when one exists, otherwise the worker's; both retry scale and flow into inputTokensPerRun, outputTokensPerRun, and roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Memory retrievals per run
entries.what: How many times one run queries the memory store: typically once at the start to load relevant history, sometimes again mid run when the agent needs older context. Each retrieval is a search that returns chunks of stored text.
entries.why: Retrieval frequency is a design choice with a multiplier attached. An agent that consults memory at every step pays the recall tax at every step, while one that loads once up front pays it once, and this field makes that difference computable.
entries.math: The first factor in memoryReadTokensPerRun = memRetrievalsPerRun times memChunksRetrieved times memChunkTokens. That total enters worker input in effectiveRolePlan and is priced at the worker's rate in roleRoutedCost, scaled by monthlyRuns like everything else per run.

entries.title: Memory chunks retrieved
entries.what: How many stored memory chunks each retrieval brings back, the memory system's top k. Like RAG retrieval depth, it controls how much remembered material gets injected per lookup.
entries.why: More recalled chunks mean richer continuity and a fatter prompt; the marginal chunk is usually less relevant than the last, so this is a diminishing returns dial. It exists separately from RAG depth because memory and document retrieval are different systems tuned independently.
entries.math: The middle factor of memoryReadTokensPerRun = memRetrievalsPerRun times memChunksRetrieved times memChunkTokens. Doubling it doubles memory input cost on every run that retrieves, flowing through effectiveRolePlan into agentMonthlyInputTokens and the routed cost.

entries.title: Tokens per memory chunk
entries.what: The token size of one stored memory chunk as it arrives in the prompt. Memory entries are usually short distilled statements, a preference, a decision, an outcome, so healthy values run far smaller than document chunks.
entries.why: Chunk size reveals memory hygiene. Systems that store raw conversation transcripts as memory carry huge chunks and pay for the sloppiness on every recall, while systems that distill before storing keep this small and their recall tax light.
entries.math: The final factor in memoryReadTokensPerRun = memRetrievalsPerRun times memChunksRetrieved times memChunkTokens. It is pure input cost: billed at the worker's input rate through effectiveRolePlan and roleRoutedCost, multiplied by monthlyRuns.

entries.title: Memory writes per run
entries.what: How many times one run saves something back to long term memory. Each write means the model generates a summary of what just happened worth remembering, then the system stores it.
entries.why: Writing is the half of memory cost that surprises people, because the generated summaries are output tokens, which every shipped provider prices at several times the input rate. An agent that journals compulsively after every step is spending premium tokens on its diary.
entries.math: Multiplies memWriteSummaryTokens to form memoryWriteTokensPerRun. In effectiveRolePlan that total is attributed as output at the summarizer's rate when summarizer calls exist, otherwise the worker's, then flows into outputTokensPerRun and roleRoutedCost.

entries.title: Summary tokens per write
entries.what: The size of each memory entry the model generates when it writes, in tokens. This is the length of the note the agent leaves for its future self: a crisp fact might be 50 tokens, a rich episode summary several hundred.
entries.why: It sets both the ongoing write bill and the future read bill, because today's write size becomes tomorrow's memChunkTokens when the entry is recalled. Disciplined, compact summaries pay for themselves twice.
entries.math: The second factor in memoryWriteTokensPerRun = memWritesPerRun times memWriteSummaryTokens. Because it is output, it lands on the expensive side of the ledger: priced at the output rate of the summarizer or worker role inside roleRoutedCost via effectiveRolePlan.

entries.title: Session traces retained
entries.what: Whether the system keeps full execution traces: every prompt, response, tool call, and result from every run, stored for audit, debugging, and compliance. A trace is the complete flight recorder of a run, not just its final answer.
entries.why: Regulated Customers often must retain traces, and retention quietly converts token volume into disk that grows every month forever. The toggle exists so the storage consequence of an audit requirement is priced instead of discovered later.
entries.math: Reveals the retention inputs and flags the report for audit posture. The volume math runs through avgBytesPerTokenInTrace in traceStoragePerMonthGB = totalMonthlyTokens times bytes per token, which accumulates into protectedStorageTB alongside replication and backup factors.

entries.title: Trace retention days
entries.what: How long each session trace must be kept before deletion, in days. The number usually comes from a compliance regime or internal audit policy rather than an engineering preference: 90 days for debugging cultures, seven years worth for regulated records.
entries.why: Retention length converts a monthly storage flow into a standing pool of data, and it is the difference between traces being a rounding error and a real line item. It also signals governance posture to the route recommendation.
entries.math: Recorded for report and compliance context; the storage accumulation in protectedStorageTB currently keys off retentionMonths in the Storage section, so keep the two aligned when a Customer states policy in days. The monthly flow it accumulates is traceStoragePerMonthGB.

entries.title: Bytes per token in trace
entries.what: How many bytes of storage one token of traffic becomes once written to the trace store. Raw text runs about four bytes per token, but real traces wrap every exchange in JSON envelopes, timestamps, metadata, and sometimes embeddings, so stored size is a multiple of text size.
entries.why: This multiplier is where trace storage estimates go wrong. Teams size for the text and then discover their observability format inflated it several times over, so making the byte rate an explicit, editable input keeps the estimate honest.
entries.math: The multiplier in traceStoragePerMonthGB = totalMonthlyTokens times avgBytesPerTokenInTrace divided by 1e9. That monthly flow accumulates over retention months inside protectedStorageTB, where growth, replication, and backup factors compound it into the capacity actually purchased.

entries.title: Planner provider
entries.what: Which model vendor family serves the planner, the role that reads the task and decomposes it into steps for the rest of the topology. Provider choice selects a whole pricing table, not just a brand.
entries.why: The planner is the brain of the run: it makes few calls but every downstream call executes its plan, so a weak planner wastes money everywhere else. Routing exists as a per role decision precisely so this one role can be premium while bulk roles go cheap.
entries.math: With the tier, it selects the rate row via findRate inside roleRoutedCost, pricing the planner's tokens from effectiveRolePlan, where replanCallsPerRun adds planner only calls on top of the base. It also shifts the providerComparison table and, when ceilingBaseline is set to selected, moves ceilingMonthly and ceilingCapex.

entries.title: Planner tier
entries.what: The capability class of the planner's model within its provider family: flagship, workhorse, or mini. Tiers abstract specific model names into three price and quality bands so routing decisions survive model releases.
entries.why: Planning is where reasoning quality earns its price: a flagship planner that produces a correct plan on the first pass is cheaper than a mini planner that triggers replans and retries across the whole run. Since planner calls are few, the premium multiplies over a small base.
entries.math: Selects the rate row with the provider via findRate in roleRoutedCost; planner input and output tokens per run, including the replanCallsPerRun surcharge, are priced at that tier's rates. A cheaper tier lowers direct planner cost but in the real system tends to raise retryRatePercent and replanRatePercent, which the topology math prices explicitly.

entries.title: Worker provider
entries.what: The vendor family serving the workers, the roles that execute the plan's steps and make the bulk of the calls in nearly every topology. Worker agents times calls per agent usually dwarfs every other call count in baseCallsPerRun.
entries.why: This is the highest leverage routing decision on the sheet because the worker rate prices more than the workers. The calculator bills all run level extras, tool results and schemas, memory reads, and retrieved RAG context, at the worker's input rate, and quick formula workload tokens are priced at the worker's rate too.
entries.math: Via findRate in roleRoutedCost, this sets the rate applied to the largest token pool from effectiveRolePlan: worker calls plus extraIn from tools, memory, and RAG. Moving it shifts the routed total, the providerComparison spread, and everything anchored to the total, including ceilingMonthly and breakEvenTokens.

entries.title: Worker tier
entries.what: The capability band for worker calls: flagship, workhorse, or mini. Workers do the routine execution, drafting, extraction, and tool wrangling, work that mid tier and small models increasingly handle well.
entries.why: The core economics of multi agent systems is that most calls do not need the best model, and workers are where that thesis pays out because their call volume is the multiplier. Downtiering workers while keeping planner and judge premium is the canonical agent cost play.
entries.math: Sets the price applied to the biggest inPerRun and outPerRun pool in roleRoutedCost, including the run level extras billed at worker rates. The route workers to the mini tier lever in optimizationLevers re runs the engine with exactly this patched and reports the monthly dollar difference.

entries.title: Judge provider
entries.what: The vendor family serving the judge, the role that evaluates worker output and decides whether it passes, needs revision, or escalates. This is the LLM as judge pattern applied inside a production run rather than in offline evaluation.
entries.why: The judge is the system's quality gate, and a judge weaker than the workers it grades approves errors it cannot see. Judges read a lot and rule briefly, so their token profile is input heavy with short outputs, keeping decent judgment affordable.
entries.math: With its tier, selects the rate row in findRate that prices judge calls from effectiveRolePlan inside roleRoutedCost. Judge calls also count in baseCallsPerRun, so they participate in retryCallsPerRun and the retryScale applied across the run.

related.label: Judging LLM as a Judge with MT Bench (Zheng et al., 2023)

entries.title: Judge tier
entries.what: The capability band for the judge role. The working rule: a judge should be at least as discerning as the models it evaluates, because grading requires spotting flaws the producer missed.
entries.why: Skimping here is the classic false economy: a mini judge rubber stamping flagship workers inverts the quality architecture, while the actual dollar exposure of a strong judge is modest because its outputs are short verdicts. Buy judgment, not verbosity.
entries.math: Prices judge tokens through findRate and roleRoutedCost. Because judge output per call is small, the tier premium lands mostly on the input side of the judge's line, a comparatively small slice of the routed total, which is why upgrading the judge rarely moves ceilingMonthly much.

related.label: Judging LLM as a Judge with MT Bench (Zheng et al., 2023)

entries.title: Summarizer provider
entries.what: The vendor family serving the summarizer, the role that compresses context between steps, condenses tool output, and writes the run's closing summaries. It appears in the routing panel only when summarizer calls per run is above zero.
entries.why: Summarization is high volume, low difficulty work, which makes it the easiest role to route cheap without quality risk. It also does double duty in the model: memory write summaries are billed at the summarizer's rate, so its pricing reaches beyond its own calls.
entries.math: Selects the rate row via findRate in roleRoutedCost for summarizer calls from effectiveRolePlan. The extraOut term, memory write tokens, is attributed to the summarizer's output rate whenever summarizerCalls is above zero, otherwise it falls to the worker.

entries.title: Summarizer tier
entries.what: The capability band for summarization calls. Compression is a task small models do nearly as well as large ones, so mini and workhorse tiers are the natural home for this role.
entries.why: Every token the summarizer saves downstream is only a win if the summarizer itself is cheap; running compression on a flagship model can quietly cancel the savings it exists to create. This dial keeps the economics of summarization honest.
entries.math: Prices summarizer input and output through findRate inside roleRoutedCost, including the memory write extraOut attributed to this role. Because summarizers emit output tokens by definition, the tier's output rate matters more here than for the judge, and downtiering shows up directly in the routed total.

entries.title: Model size quick pick
entries.what: A shortcut menu of common open model parameter counts, from 8B up to 405B. Picking one writes that number into the Model parameters field below so you do not have to remember that a Llama 70B means 70 billion parameters. Choose custom to type any value yourself.
entries.why: Architects and Customers talk in named sizes, not raw numbers. The quick pick maps the models people actually deploy onto the one number the sizing math cares about, which keeps a live meeting moving instead of stalling on a spec lookup.
entries.math: This field only sets modelParamsB; it has no formula of its own. Whatever it writes flows straight into modelWeightMemoryGB, which is modelParamsB times bytes per parameter, and from there into every downstream memory and GPU count result. Change the pick and the entire sizing stack recomputes.

entries.title: Model parameters (billions)
entries.what: The total number of learned weights in the model, expressed in billions. A 70B model has roughly 70 billion parameters, each of which must sit in GPU memory as a number of some precision before the model can serve a single token.
entries.why: Parameter count is the single biggest driver of how much hardware a private deployment needs. It sets the memory floor and correlates strongly with both quality and per token latency, so it is the first number any sizing conversation must pin down.
entries.math: Feeds modelWeightMemoryGB directly: parameters in billions times bytes per parameter from the quantization choice. That result then flows into runtimeOverheadGB, safetyMarginGB, totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB, and finally gpusRequiredByMemory. It also picks which benchmark class (8B or 70B) gpusRequiredByThroughput uses for its default TPS estimate.

entries.title: Quantization
entries.what: The numeric precision used to store each model weight. FP16 and BF16 use 2 bytes per parameter, FP8 and INT8 use 1 byte, and INT4 uses half a byte. Lower precision shrinks the model in memory, usually with some quality cost that varies by model and task.
entries.why: Quantization is the cheapest lever for fitting a big model on less hardware. Dropping a 70B model from FP16 to INT4 cuts its weight footprint from 140 GB to 35 GB, which can be the difference between one GPU and four. The tradeoff is accuracy risk that must be validated on the Customer's actual workload.
entries.math: Sets bytes per parameter inside modelWeightMemoryGB, which is modelParamsB times that byte count. Halving the bytes halves the weights term in totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB, and since runtimeOverheadGB and safetyMarginGB scale off that stack, the effect on gpusRequiredByMemory is larger than the weights alone suggest.

entries.title: Model instances
entries.what: How many independent copies of the model you run. Each instance is a full serving replica with its own weights, KV cache, and overhead. Teams run multiple instances for redundancy, for isolation between workloads, or to scale request throughput horizontally.
entries.why: One instance is a single point of failure and a single queue. Production Customers usually want at least two for availability, and busy platforms shard traffic across many. Every extra instance repeats the whole memory bill, so this multiplier deserves a deliberate choice, not a default.
entries.math: Multiplies the memory gate: gpusRequiredByMemory is the ceiling of totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB over usableVramPerGpuGB, times modelInstances. It does not multiply the throughput gate, since gpusRequiredByThroughput already sizes total capacity. The larger gate wins in recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: GPU candidate
entries.what: The GPU model you are sizing against, chosen from a hardware profile list. Each profile carries the card's VRAM capacity and a conservative default output tokens per second benchmark for an 8B class and a 70B class model.
entries.why: The GPU is the unit everything else is counted in. Its VRAM decides how many cards the model needs to fit, and its throughput decides how many cards the traffic needs. Comparing candidates here is how you test whether a bigger memory card beats more smaller cards for a given Customer workload.
entries.math: Supplies gpuVramGB to usableVramPerGpuGB (VRAM times the memory utilization target), which sets gpusRequiredByMemory. It also supplies the default benchmarkTpsPerGpu inside gpusRequiredByThroughput unless userBenchmarkTpsPerGpu overrides it. Both gates then compete in recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: Concurrent sequences
entries.what: How many generation streams the serving engine holds in flight at the same time. Every active request the model is currently answering is a sequence, and each one keeps its own KV cache resident in GPU memory for its whole lifetime.
entries.why: Concurrency, not model size, is what usually blows up memory budgets in production. A model that fits fine serving one user can run out of VRAM serving forty, because each added sequence claims a full context worth of KV cache. This input turns user load into a memory requirement.
entries.math: First multiplier in kvCacheServingGB: concurrent sequences times context length times layers times KV heads times head dimension times 2 tensors times bytes per element. That KV term joins weights and overhead in totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB, so doubling concurrency can flip the winning gate in recommendedGpuCount from throughput to memory.

entries.title: Context length (tokens)
entries.what: The maximum number of tokens one sequence can hold, covering the prompt, retrieved context, tool output, and the generated reply combined. The serving engine reserves KV cache for this window per concurrent sequence.
entries.why: Long context is the most seductive and most expensive feature in modern serving. Agents that stuff retrieval results and history into the prompt push this number up fast, and KV memory grows linearly with it. Sizing to the real working window instead of the model's advertised maximum saves real hardware.
entries.math: Multiplies straight through kvCacheServingGB alongside concurrentSequences. Since KV cache is often the largest term in totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB for concurrent serving, context length is frequently the deciding factor in gpusRequiredByMemory and therefore in recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: Model layers
entries.what: The number of transformer layers in the model architecture. Every layer maintains its own slice of KV cache for every token of every active sequence, so this is a per architecture constant you read off the model card, not a tuning knob.
entries.why: KV cache math is architecture math. An 80 layer 70B model and a 32 layer 8B model store very different amounts of attention state per token, and the calculator cannot know which one you picked unless you tell it. The default describes a 70B class dense model; edit it to match the real model.
entries.math: Linear multiplier inside kvCacheServingGB: sequences times context times kvLayers times kvHeads times kvHeadDim times 2 times bytes per element. Getting layers wrong scales the entire KV estimate by the same ratio, which propagates through totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB into gpusRequiredByMemory.

entries.title: KV heads
entries.what: The number of key value attention heads the model stores state for. Modern models use grouped query attention, where many query heads share a few KV heads, so this number is usually far smaller than the total attention head count. A 70B class model typically has 8 KV heads against 64 query heads.
entries.why: This is where recent architectures quietly got cheap to serve. Grouped query attention cut KV memory by 8x or more versus older full multi head designs, and that saving is exactly what makes long context concurrent serving feasible. Read the value from the model card; guessing the query head count instead inflates KV memory dramatically.
entries.math: Linear multiplier inside kvCacheServingGB. Because kvHeads is the term that grouped query attention shrinks, entering 64 where the model actually uses 8 overstates KV cache eightfold and can push gpusRequiredByMemory, and thus recommendedGpuCount, to an absurd number.

entries.title: Head dimension
entries.what: The width of each attention head, meaning how many numbers each head stores per token for its key and again for its value. 128 is the common figure for current large models. Like layers and KV heads, this comes from the model architecture.
entries.why: It completes the per token KV footprint: layers times KV heads times head dimension times two tensors times bytes per element is exactly how many bytes one token of context costs in cache. Knowing this shape lets you sanity check any vendor's memory claims yourself.
entries.math: Linear multiplier inside kvCacheServingGB, which then feeds totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB and gpusRequiredByMemory. Most models cluster near 128, so this input moves results less often than layers or KV heads, but an unusual architecture still needs the real value.

entries.title: KV bytes per element
entries.what: The storage precision of the KV cache itself, separate from the weight quantization above. FP16 KV cache costs 2 bytes per element; FP8 KV cache costs 1; some engines support even lower. Serving engines let you set KV precision independently because cache dominates memory at high concurrency.
entries.why: Quantizing the cache is the second half of the memory story. A deployment that quantized weights to INT4 but left KV at FP16 can still be cache bound. Halving KV precision halves the biggest memory term in long context serving, usually with modest quality impact, which makes this a high leverage and frequently forgotten knob.
entries.math: Final multiplier in kvCacheServingGB. Cutting it from 2 to 1 halves the KV term inside totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB, and since safetyMarginGB is computed on top of that stack, the total saving compounds slightly before reaching gpusRequiredByMemory.

entries.title: Runtime overhead percent
entries.what: Extra GPU memory the serving engine itself consumes beyond weights and KV cache, expressed as a percent of weight memory. CUDA graphs, activation workspaces, compiled kernels, and framework buffers all claim real VRAM before a single request arrives. The default is 15 percent.
entries.why: Sizing from weights plus cache alone is how deployments fail on first boot. Every inference engine reserves working memory, and the amount varies by engine and configuration, so the calculator makes it an explicit visible line instead of hiding it inside a fudge factor.
entries.math: Computes runtimeOverheadGB as modelWeightMemoryGB times this percent. That term joins weights and kvCacheServingGB inside totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB and is also part of the base that safetyMarginGB multiplies, so it influences gpusRequiredByMemory twice over.

entries.title: Safety margin percent
entries.what: Deliberate headroom added on top of weights, KV cache, and runtime overhead, as a percent of their sum. It absorbs memory fragmentation, transient allocation spikes, and the gap between clean benchmark conditions and messy production traffic. The default is 20 percent.
entries.why: Sizing a GPU to 100 percent of calculated need is sizing it to fail, because the calculation is an estimate and production is not steady. The margin is the honest admission that real systems need slack, and making it a visible percent lets a Customer decide how much slack they are buying rather than inheriting it silently.
entries.math: Computes safetyMarginGB as the sum of modelWeightMemoryGB, kvCacheServingGB, and runtimeOverheadGB, times this percent. It is the last term added into totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB, so raising it pushes gpusRequiredByMemory up whenever the total sits near a per GPU boundary.

entries.title: GPU memory utilization target
entries.what: The fraction of a GPU's physical VRAM the serving engine is allowed to allocate, written as a decimal like 0.85. Engines such as vLLM expose this directly as a launch parameter; the remainder stays free for the driver, other processes, and allocation churn.
entries.why: A GPU with 80 GB of raw VRAM does not offer 80 GB to your model. Some fraction must stay unclaimed or the process risks out of memory crashes under load. This target converts the card on the spec sheet into the card you can actually use, which is the honest denominator for counting GPUs.
entries.math: Computes usableVramPerGpuGB as gpuVramGB times this target. That usable figure is the denominator in gpusRequiredByMemory, so lowering the target from 0.90 to 0.80 can add a whole GPU when totalGpuMemoryRequiredGB sits near a card boundary.

entries.title: Target GPU utilization
entries.what: The fraction of a GPU's benchmark throughput you plan to actually consume at peak, written as a decimal like 0.70. It is a planning discount, distinct from the memory utilization target above: this one is about compute headroom, not VRAM.
entries.why: Systems run at 100 percent utilization have no room for traffic spikes, garbage collection pauses, or batch scheduling inefficiency, and their latency degrades sharply as queues form. Planning to 70 percent is standard capacity discipline: it buys predictable latency at the cost of some idle silicon.
entries.math: Discounts the denominator in gpusRequiredByThroughput, which is the ceiling of requiredPeakOutputTps over benchmarkTpsPerGpu times targetGpuUtilization. Lowering it inflates the throughput bound GPU count, which competes with gpusRequiredByMemory inside recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: Measured benchmark TPS per GPU
entries.what: A throughput number you measured yourself: output tokens per second one GPU sustains serving your model at your context length and concurrency. When filled in, it replaces the conservative labeled estimate that ships with the GPU profile. Leave it blank to keep the estimate.
entries.why: Published throughput figures are marketing conditions, and the shipped defaults here are deliberately conservative estimates, not measurements of your workload. A one day rented GPU benchmark produces the only number worth committing capital against, which is why the calculator flags estimated throughput with a warning until you override it.
entries.math: Overrides benchmarkTpsPerGpu inside gpusRequiredByThroughput: the ceiling of requiredPeakOutputTps over benchmark TPS times targetGpuUtilization. A real measurement above the default shrinks the throughput bound count; below it, the count grows and may overtake gpusRequiredByMemory in recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: Target completion seconds
entries.what: How many seconds a user should wait for one full agent run to finish generating at peak load. It is the latency promise you are sizing to: a tighter target means the hardware must produce the same tokens in less time.
entries.why: Peak throughput requirements are meaningless without a time bound. Forty concurrent runs needing 4,000 output tokens each is a gentle load spread over two minutes and a brutal one squeezed into ten seconds. This field is where the Customer's patience becomes an engineering requirement.
entries.math: Denominator in both requiredPeakOutputTps (peak concurrent runs times outputTokensPerRun divided by this) and requiredRequestsPerSecond. Halving it doubles required peak TPS, which flows into gpusRequiredByThroughput and can flip the winning gate in recommendedGpuCount.

entries.title: Minimum platform GPU count
entries.what: A floor on the GPU count regardless of what the memory and throughput math says. Platforms sell in fixed node sizes, high availability designs need spare capacity, and some vendor architectures simply start at four or eight GPUs per node.
entries.why: The math can honestly conclude that two GPUs suffice while the only buyable configuration contains eight. Real procurement happens in platform granularity, not single cards, and encoding that floor keeps the recommendation purchasable instead of theoretical.
entries.math: Third argument in recommendedGpuCount, which takes the maximum of gpusRequiredByMemory, gpusRequiredByThroughput, and this floor. When it wins, the result card labels the outcome platform minimum bound, telling you the workload is smaller than the smallest box that runs it.

entries.title: Model repository TB
entries.what: Disk space for storing model artifacts: the serving model in each quantization you keep, previous versions for rollback, fine tuned variants, and any candidate models under evaluation. A single 70B model at FP16 is roughly 140 GB before you keep a second copy of anything.
entries.why: Model files are the forgotten storage line. Teams accumulate checkpoints and variants fast, and a repository that started at half a terabyte quietly becomes five. Rollback discipline requires keeping old versions, so this grows with operational maturity, not just with model count.
entries.math: First term in the rawStorageTB sum inside protectedStorageTB, alongside vector database growth and trace storage at retention. The whole sum then gets multiplied by growth, replicationFactor, and backupFactor, so every repository terabyte becomes several purchased terabytes.

entries.title: Retention months
entries.what: How many months of accumulated data you keep before deleting: session traces, audit logs, and vector index growth all pile up month over month until retention expires. Compliance regimes commonly dictate this number rather than engineering preference.
entries.why: Retention converts a monthly flow into a standing stock. Keeping 12 months instead of 3 quadruples the trace and vector storage you must provision, and regulated Customers often cannot choose the shorter number. This is where audit requirements become disk purchases.
entries.math: Multiplies both accumulating terms inside protectedStorageTB: vector database growth (vectorDbLegacyGB times 30 days times retention months) and trace storage (traceStoragePerMonthGB times retention months). The model repository term is unaffected since weights do not accumulate with time.

entries.title: Growth percent
entries.what: Expected growth in storage demand over the planning horizon, applied as a single uplift on the raw capacity total. It covers new document sources, rising usage, and the universal tendency of data estates to expand faster than predicted.
entries.why: Buying storage for today's footprint guarantees a procurement crisis later, and storage expansions on integrated platforms are rarely instant. Provisioning growth up front is cheaper than emergency expansion, and making the assumption explicit lets the Customer challenge it.
entries.math: Applied inside protectedStorageTB as rawStorageTB times one plus this percent, before replication and backup multiply the result. Because it stacks multiplicatively with replicationFactor and backupFactor, 30 percent growth can add far more purchased capacity than it first appears.

entries.title: Replication factor
entries.what: How many live copies of the data the storage layer maintains for resilience. A factor of 2 means every byte exists twice on independent hardware; 3 is common for distributed storage systems that must survive node failures without data loss.
entries.why: Raw capacity is never what gets bought. A storage cluster quoting 100 TB usable at replication factor 2 contains 200 TB of physical disk, and someone pays for the physical number. Surfacing this multiplier keeps the storage line of a quote from looking mysteriously inflated.
entries.math: Direct multiplier in protectedStorageTB: raw storage times growth uplift times replicationFactor times backupFactor. Moving from 2 to 3 adds 50 percent to the purchased capacity in one step, which makes it one of the most powerful single inputs in the storage section.

entries.title: Backup factor
entries.what: A multiplier for backup capacity on top of live replicated storage. A factor of 1.5 means backups consume an extra half of the protected footprint, accounting for snapshot chains, backup copies, and recovery points held outside the primary system.
entries.why: Replication protects against hardware failure; backups protect against deletion, corruption, and ransomware, and they are separate capacity. Customers with audit obligations almost always need both, and quoting storage without the backup tier is how projects come back for embarrassing second purchases.
entries.math: Final multiplier in protectedStorageTB, stacking on growth and replication. Because it applies last, it scales everything: model repository, vector index at retention, and traces all get the backup uplift together.

entries.title: Average bytes per token
entries.what: How many bytes of network payload one generated token occupies when streamed to the user. Plain English text averages about 4 bytes per token, but JSON wrappers, server sent event framing, and markup push the wire figure higher than the raw text.
entries.why: Token streams feel weightless until you multiply them by peak concurrency. This constant converts the throughput math into bandwidth math, letting the network conversation use the same peak numbers the GPU conversation already established rather than a separate guess.
entries.math: Multiplier in userResponseBandwidthMbps: requiredPeakOutputTps times bytes per token times 8 bits, in megabits. That result is the first term in totalApplicationNetworkMbps, the front side network requirement shown beside the rule based backend fabric guidance.

entries.title: Retrieved bytes per RAG query
entries.what: The network payload one retrieval query pulls from the vector store and document sources, in bytes. It includes the retrieved chunks, their metadata, and protocol overhead, and it is usually far larger than the token stream the user eventually sees.
entries.why: Retrieval payloads dwarf token streams. A query returning ten chunks with metadata can move a hundred kilobytes while the model's answer streams a few. On busy RAG systems this traffic, not the responses, sizes the application network, and it flows between internal systems where bandwidth is assumed rather than planned.
entries.math: Multiplier in ragTrafficMbps: peak RAG queries per second (requiredRequestsPerSecond times ragCallsPerRun) times bytes per query times 8 bits. Feeds totalApplicationNetworkMbps alongside user and tool traffic.

entries.title: Tool payload bytes per call
entries.what: The average network payload of one tool call round trip, in bytes: the request the agent sends to an external system plus the response that comes back. Database queries, API calls, and document fetches all count.
entries.why: Agentic systems are chatty. An agent making eight tool calls per run at high concurrency generates a steady mesh of internal traffic that nobody provisioned, because tool calls were nobody's line item. This field makes that traffic visible next to the token math it accompanies.
entries.math: Multiplier in toolTrafficMbps: peak tool calls per second (requiredRequestsPerSecond times toolCallsPerRun) times payload bytes times 8 bits. Third term in totalApplicationNetworkMbps.

entries.title: Overhead Mbps
entries.what: A flat bandwidth allowance for everything the per flow math does not capture: TLS handshakes, health checks, monitoring and log shipping, retries, and management traffic. Entered directly in megabits per second rather than derived.
entries.why: Real networks carry a constant hum of traffic beside the payload flows, and pretending otherwise produces links that saturate below their calculated load. A flat overhead line is a blunt instrument, but an honest and visible one that a Customer's network team can adjust from their own telemetry.
entries.math: Added as the final term in totalApplicationNetworkMbps, after userResponseBandwidthMbps, ragTrafficMbps, and toolTrafficMbps. It shifts the front side total up by a constant; the backend GPU fabric guidance beside the card is unaffected.

entries.title: Ceiling baseline
entries.what: Which provider cost the hardware ceiling is measured against: the cheapest single provider family total, or the total of your actual selected role routing. The ceiling says owned hardware must beat tokens by your margin, and this picks which token bill counts as tokens.
entries.why: The comparison is only as honest as its baseline. Measuring against your possibly premium role routing flatters hardware; measuring against the cheapest provider is the stricter test, because the cheapest provider is your real alternative if cost is the deciding factor. The calculator defaults to strict and lets you choose knowingly.
entries.math: Selects the providerMonthlyCost fed into hardwareCeiling: the comparison minimum from providerComparison, or the roleRoutedCost total when set to selected. Everything downstream shifts with it: ceilingMonthly, ceilingCapex, the quote verdict, breakEvenMillionTokens, and the financeDecision headline.

entries.title: Required savings threshold percent
entries.what: How much cheaper than the token route owned hardware must be before cost alone can recommend buying it, as a percent. The default of 40 means ownership needs to land at 60 percent of the provider bill or less. The calculator clamps this to a maximum of 90.
entries.why: Ownership is not free even when the invoice is smaller: it adds capacity planning, operational staffing, failure risk, and lifecycle burden that token pricing quietly includes. The threshold prices that burden as a visible rule instead of a hidden bias, and a Customer with strong ops maturity can lower it deliberately.
entries.math: Sets the ceiling: ceilingMonthly equals providerMonthlyCost times one minus this percent, and ceilingCapex is that times usefulLifeMonths. It also decides financeDecision verdicts, separating buy from negotiate, and shapes the default budget inside breakEvenMillionTokens when no quote is entered.

entries.title: Useful life months
entries.what: How many months the purchased hardware realistically serves before replacement, typically 36 to 60 for GPU systems. It is the horizon over which a one time purchase gets spread into a monthly figure comparable with a token bill.
entries.why: Capex and opex cannot be compared until they share a time base. GPU useful life is genuinely uncertain: new silicon generations can strand old hardware economically before it fails physically, so an optimistic 60 months flatters ownership while a cautious 36 protects the Customer. The choice deserves to be argued, not assumed.
entries.math: Multiplies ceilingMonthly into ceilingCapex, the all in number a quote must beat. It also amortizes your quote into a monthly budget inside breakEvenMillionTokens, and sets the comparison horizon in financeDecision where total token spend is providerMonthlyCost times this horizon.

entries.title: Hardware quote (USD, optional)
entries.what: A real all in hardware price from a vendor, in dollars. TokenOps never prices hardware itself by design; it derives the ceiling the hardware must come under, and this field is where you hold an actual quote against that ceiling for an instant verdict.
entries.why: The ceiling is a negotiating number and a quote is what gets negotiated. Entering one turns abstract economics into a decision: under the ceiling means ownership beats tokens by your required margin, over means it does not. The field also refuses implausible entries, because a one dollar quote would produce confident nonsense.
entries.math: Compared against ceilingCapex from hardwareCeiling to produce the under or over verdict, with a plausibility floor of 2 percent of the ceiling or 1,000 dollars. When present it replaces the derived ceiling as the monthly budget in breakEvenMillionTokens, and it is the principal that financeDecision amortizes or finances into a monthly payment.

entries.title: Rented GPU hourly price (USD, optional)
entries.what: The hourly rental price for one GPU from a cloud GPU provider, in dollars. Entering a value here activates the rented GPU cost path, a third option between paying per token and buying hardware. TokenOps ships no rental price defaults; this is always your number from a real provider page.
entries.why: Renting is the de risking move the buy versus tokens debate usually skips. A short rental produces the measured benchmark that userBenchmarkTpsPerGpu wants and real usage data, all before anyone signs a hardware purchase order. For spiky workloads, renting active hours can also simply win outright.
entries.math: First factor in rentedGpuMonthlyCost: hourly price times rentedGpuCount times rentedActiveHoursPerMonth plus storage, network, and platform fees. The same trace derives a validation project cost and a rented break even in managed million tokens per month for comparison against the token route.

entries.title: Rented GPU count
entries.what: How many GPUs you rent simultaneously. For a serious validation this should match the recommendedGpuCount from the sizing section, so the rented cluster actually resembles the configuration you are considering buying.
entries.why: Benchmarking one rented GPU and extrapolating to eight hides the multi GPU realities that decide production behavior: tensor parallel communication, batch scheduling across cards, and interconnect limits. Renting the honest count costs more per hour and saves you from discovering scaling losses after the purchase order clears.
entries.math: Linear multiplier in rentedGpuMonthlyCost alongside the hourly price and active hours, and in the validation project cost computed in the same trace. Doubling the count doubles the GPU portion of the rental bill while fixed storage and platform fees stay put.

entries.title: Rented active hours per month
entries.what: How many hours per month the rented GPUs actually run. Around 730 hours is always on; a business hours workload might justify 200. Most rental providers bill only for provisioned time, so idle hours you deprovision are money kept.
entries.why: This field is the rental model's entire advantage. Owned hardware costs the same whether it works or idles, while rentals scale with use, so bursty workloads can beat both tokens and ownership by renting only the active window. Watching this number swing the monthly cost teaches the usage pattern lesson faster than any slide.
entries.math: Third factor in rentedGpuMonthlyCost: hourly price times GPU count times active hours, plus fixed monthly fees. Low hours shrink the rented total toward the fixed fees; at always on hours, compare the result against ceilingMonthly to see whether owning starts to dominate.

entries.title: Finance mode
entries.what: How the hardware purchase is paid for in the ownership math: cash, where the quote is simply spread evenly across the term, or financed, where a standard loan payment formula applies your APR over the term. It changes the shape and total of the cost, not the hardware.
entries.why: Most Customers do not pay cash for infrastructure, and a cost model that assumes they do understates the real monthly burden. Financing preserves capital at the price of interest, and the right choice depends on the Customer's cost of capital, which is a CFO conversation this toggle makes concrete.
entries.math: Branches the payment calculation in financeDecision: cash gives quote divided by term months, financed gives the amortizing loan payment at the monthly rate derived from financeAprPercent. The resulting payment is compared against ceilingMonthly and providerMonthlyCost to produce the buy, negotiate, or stay on tokens verdict.

entries.title: Finance term months
entries.what: The repayment period for the hardware purchase, from 12 to 60 months on the slider. In cash mode it is the amortization window; in financed mode it is the loan term the payment formula runs over.
entries.why: Term length is the classic payment versus total cost tradeoff. Stretching from 36 to 60 months shrinks the monthly payment, which can flip a verdict from negotiate to buy, while increasing total interest paid in financed mode. A term that outlives the hardware's useful life means paying for silicon that has already been retired.
entries.math: Divides the quote into the monthly payment in financeDecision, directly in cash mode and through the loan formula in financed mode. It also extends the comparison horizon, which financeDecision takes as the maximum of this term and usefulLifeMonths when totaling token spend against hardware spend.

entries.title: Finance APR percent
entries.what: The annual percentage rate on the financing, from 0 to 15 percent on the slider. It is the yearly cost of borrowing, converted internally to a monthly rate for the standard amortizing payment formula. It only matters when finance mode is set to financed.
entries.why: Interest is a real cost of ownership that cash comparisons ignore. At meaningful rates over multi year terms, financing adds a double digit percentage to the effective hardware cost, which can erase a savings margin that looked comfortable at the sticker price. The Customer's actual borrowing rate belongs here, not a guess.
entries.math: Sets the monthly rate r inside financeDecision's loan payment formula: quote times r times one plus r to the term power, over one plus r to the term power minus one. Higher APR raises the payment, which is then tested against ceilingMonthly for the buy verdict and against providerMonthlyCost for the tokens verdict.

entries.title: Time to value need
entries.what: How urgently the Customer needs a working result in production, rated on a scale of none, low, medium, high. It measures schedule pressure, not technical difficulty. A pilot that must demo to the board next month is high; a research initiative with no deadline is low.
entries.why: Speed changes the right answer. Managed API routes ship in days because someone else runs the infrastructure, while owned hardware takes procurement cycles measured in months. Capturing urgency as an explicit input keeps the schedule argument visible instead of buried in someone's head.
entries.math: This Likert value (0 to 3) is multiplied by its weight in two route scores inside scoreRoutes: the direct provider route and the Airia platform route. Higher values raise those routes' raw component points, which are then normalized against routeMax to produce the 0 to 100 score you see in the scoring panel. It never touches token or cost math.

entries.title: Model quality need
entries.what: How much the workload depends on frontier level model capability. High means the task fails on anything less than the best available models, for example complex reasoning, nuanced writing, or agentic coding. Low means a small open model would do the job.
entries.why: Frontier models live behind provider APIs first and reach private deployment later or never. If the Customer genuinely needs top capability, private routes carry a quality penalty that no amount of hardware fixes. Stating this need up front prevents sizing a private cluster for a workload the private model cannot actually serve.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the direct route weight in scoreRoutes, pushing the recommendation toward the direct provider route. It pairs with modelQualityConfidence, which reports how sure you are that a private model would suffice. It does not change monthlyRuns or any cost formula.

entries.title: Low operations burden need
entries.what: How strongly the Customer wants someone else to run the machinery. High means no appetite for patching serving engines, managing GPU drivers, or paging an engineer at 3am. This is about who carries the operational load, not whether the system works.
entries.why: Owning inference hardware is a standing operational commitment: monitoring, upgrades, capacity management, failure handling. Many Customers underestimate this and buy hardware they cannot staff. This field forces the conversation before the purchase order, not after.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the direct route's needLowOps weight in scoreRoutes, raising the managed API route's raw score. It is the mirror image of opsReadiness, which rewards the owned hardware and HPE routes instead. Neither field touches the token demand chain.

entries.title: Usage pattern
entries.what: Whether demand arrives in bursts or flows steadily. Bursty means sharp peaks and long idle valleys, like a sales team hammering an assistant before quarter close. Steady means near constant load, like a background document pipeline running around the clock.
entries.why: Hardware you own costs the same whether it is busy or idle, so owned GPUs only pay off when they stay busy. Per token pricing absorbs bursts without waste. This single toggle captures the core economic difference between renting capacity and owning it.
entries.math: Bursty adds the burstyBonus weight to the direct route; steady adds the steadyUsage weight to the owned hardware route in scoreRoutes. It also frames how to read ceilingMonthly: a steady workload can amortize a hardware quote across every hour, while a bursty one leaves the amortized cost stranded in idle time.

entries.title: Governance need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs enforced control over AI usage: approved model catalogs, usage policies, spend limits, and role based access applied by the platform rather than by trust. High means governance must be a product feature, not a memo.
entries.why: Once more than a handful of teams use AI, ungoverned access becomes shadow IT with a token bill. Platforms that enforce governance centrally solve a real enterprise problem that raw provider APIs do not. This field separates Customers who need that layer from those who just need an endpoint.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies governance weights in two route scores inside scoreRoutes: the Airia platform route and the HPE Private Cloud AI route. It is distinct from needGovernancePlanning, which scores the advisory route for Customers who need help designing governance rather than software that enforces it.

entries.title: Agent builder need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs a platform where teams assemble agents from building blocks instead of writing orchestration code from scratch. Think visual flows, prebuilt connectors, and managed deployment rather than a custom Python codebase per agent.
entries.why: Custom agent code is powerful but expensive to maintain, and most enterprises cannot staff a framework team per use case. A builder platform trades flexibility for speed and repeatability. Rating this need tells the calculator whether platform routes deserve extra points.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the Airia route's needAgentBuilder weight in scoreRoutes, raising that route's raw score before normalization. It has no effect on baseCallsPerRun or any token formula; the agent topology section models what the agents do, this field models who gets to build them.

entries.title: Integration need
entries.what: How much the agents must connect to existing business systems: CRM, ticketing, ERP, internal databases. High means the value of the agent is mostly in the systems it reads from and writes to, not in freestanding chat.
entries.why: Integration is where agent projects live or die. An assistant that cannot touch the systems of record is a demo; one that can is a workflow. Platforms with prebuilt connectors earn their keep exactly here, so the route scoring needs to know how much this matters.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the Airia route's needIntegration weight in scoreRoutes. At medium or above it also triggers discoveryQuestions to ask which systems the agent will read from and write to. The actual token cost of integration shows up separately through toolSchemaOverheadPerCall and toolResultTokensPerRun in the tools section.

entries.title: Model routing need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs to send different tasks to different models: cheap models for simple steps, premium models for hard ones, possibly across multiple providers. High means one size fits all model choice is off the table.
entries.why: Routing is where agent economics get won. A planner on a frontier model with workers on a budget tier can cut the bill severalfold at equal quality, but it requires infrastructure that can switch models per role. Customers who need this should be steered toward routes that support it.
entries.math: This value fires three times in scoreRoutes: once in the Airia route and twice in the hybrid route, where both the needModelRouting and routeFlexibility weights scale by it. It is the philosophy behind effectiveRolePlan and roleRoutedCost, where every role carries its own provider and tier and the cost engine prices each role at its own rate.

entries.title: Audit need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs a durable, reviewable record of what the agents did: every prompt, response, tool call, and decision, retained and searchable. High usually means a regulator or internal compliance function will eventually ask to see it.
entries.why: Auditability is easy to bolt on in a slide and hard to bolt on in production. Platforms that log everything natively save a painful retrofit. It also has a physical cost: retained traces turn tokens into disk, which the storage formulas make visible.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the Airia route's needAudit weight in scoreRoutes. Its storage consequence is computed separately: traceStoragePerMonthGB multiplies totalMonthlyTokens by bytes per token in the trace, and retention months roll that into protectedStorageTB. High audit need with long retention moves real terabytes.

entries.title: Business user agent creation
entries.what: How much the Customer wants people outside engineering to create and modify agents themselves. High means analysts and operations staff should build agents through a guided interface, with IT providing guardrails instead of writing every agent by hand.
entries.why: The demand for agents inside an enterprise always outruns the developer headcount available to build them. Letting business users self serve is how adoption scales past the pilot team, but it only works on platforms designed for it. This field flags that requirement early.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the Airia route's needBusinessUserCreation weight in scoreRoutes, adding raw points that are normalized against routeMax. It has no effect on the demand model; whoever builds the agent, monthlyRuns and totalCallsPerRun price it the same.

entries.title: Data gravity
entries.what: How strongly the Customer's data resists moving. Large, regulated, or deeply integrated datasets pull compute toward themselves because copying them out is slow, expensive, or forbidden. High means the model must come to the data, not the reverse.
entries.why: Data gravity is the quiet force behind most private AI deployments. It is not always a policy rule; sometimes ten petabytes of operational data simply cannot be shipped to an API endpoint with acceptable latency or cost. Naming it as an input keeps this physical constraint in the route decision.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the Kamiwaza route's needDataGravity weight in scoreRoutes, since that platform's premise is running inference next to the data. It compounds with privatePolicyScore, which captures the policy version of the same pressure. Neither alters token counts; they redirect where those tokens get processed.

entries.title: Ontology driven context
entries.what: How much the Customer needs a structured semantic model of their business, entities, relationships, and definitions, to drive what context reaches the model. Instead of retrieving raw text chunks, an ontology driven system retrieves meaning: this Customer, owns that contract, governed by this policy.
entries.why: Plain vector retrieval breaks down when the question spans relationships that no single document states. Enterprises with complex domains, insurance, defense, logistics, often need the knowledge graph layer before RAG becomes trustworthy. This field identifies those Customers.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the Kamiwaza route's needOntology weight in scoreRoutes. It is a route driver only; the token cost of whatever retrieval approach wins is modeled in the RAG section through retrievedContextTokens and its downstream lines.

entries.title: Internal API need
entries.what: How much the agents must call APIs that exist only inside the Customer's network: internal microservices, mainframe wrappers, plant systems. High means the useful tools are unreachable from any public cloud without opening the perimeter.
entries.why: An agent hosted outside the network cannot call an API that never leaves it, so this requirement quietly eliminates whole categories of architecture. Surfacing it as a scored input makes that elimination explicit instead of discovering it during the security review.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the Kamiwaza route's needInternalApis weight in scoreRoutes, favoring platforms deployed inside the perimeter. The traffic cost of those calls is modeled independently in the tools section and in toolTrafficMbps on the network side.

entries.title: Enterprise retrieval need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs search across their whole document estate, SharePoint, wikis, file shares, ticket history, with permissions enforced per user. This is RAG at enterprise scale: not one indexed folder but the organization's memory, access controlled.
entries.why: Retrieval quality is the ceiling on assistant quality; the model can only reason over what reaches its context. Permission aware retrieval across messy enterprise sources is genuinely hard and is a differentiator between platforms. Customers who need it should be routed to platforms built for it.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the Kamiwaza route's needEnterpriseRetrieval weight in scoreRoutes. Its cost shadow lives in the RAG formulas: retrievedContextTokens per query, monthlyEmbeddingTokens for index churn, and vectorRecords for store sizing all scale with how seriously retrieval is used.

entries.title: AI readiness gap
entries.what: The distance between what the Customer wants to do with AI and what their people, data, and processes can support today. High means ambition far exceeds current capability: no data hygiene, no AI literate staff, no operating model for deploying models safely.
entries.why: Selling infrastructure into a readiness gap produces expensive shelfware. Some Customers need an advisory engagement before they need a single GPU, and an honest sizing tool says so. This field is how the calculator earns the right to recommend consulting instead of hardware.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the BTG advisory route's readinessGap weight in scoreRoutes. When readiness gap and its advisory siblings dominate, the advisory route outscores every execution route, which is the tool telling you the Customer is not ready to size. It never touches cost formulas.

entries.title: Use case ambiguity
entries.what: How unclear the actual use case still is. High means the Customer wants AI but cannot yet name the workflow, the user, or the success metric. Low means a specific process with a specific owner is on the table.
entries.why: Every number in this calculator downstream of an ambiguous use case is fiction with decimals. Ambiguity is not a sin, it is a stage, but it should route toward discovery work rather than hardware sizing. Scoring it keeps the tool honest about what it does not know.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the BTG advisory route's useCaseAmbiguity weight in scoreRoutes. It works alongside checkDoNotSize, which refuses to produce a recommendation at all when the policy gate, usage volume, or budget signal are missing entirely. High ambiguity plus missing gates means discovery, not sizing.

entries.title: Vendor selection need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs structured help choosing among vendors and platforms: evaluation criteria, bake offs, reference checks, contract posture. High means they are staring at a crowded market with no internal method for cutting through it.
entries.why: Vendor selection done badly locks in years of cost and constraint. Customers who recognize they need selection help are asking for an advisory engagement, not a quote, and the routing should honor that. It also signals the deal is earlier stage than the technical inputs might suggest.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the BTG advisory route's needVendorSelection weight in scoreRoutes, raising the advisory route's raw score before normalization against routeMax. No cost or demand formula reads it.

entries.title: Integration complexity
entries.what: How tangled the Customer's existing systems landscape is: legacy platforms, custom middleware, half finished migrations, undocumented interfaces. This rates the terrain the AI project must cross, independent of how much integration the agents themselves need.
entries.why: A simple agent dropped into a complex landscape becomes a complex project. High complexity predicts long timelines and argues for advisory led scoping before anyone commits to architecture. It is deliberately separate from needIntegration, which measures desired connectivity rather than environmental difficulty.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the BTG advisory route's integrationComplexity weight in scoreRoutes, pushing toward an advisory first engagement. It does not feed the tools section token math; that models the agent's own tool calls, not the surrounding landscape.

entries.title: Governance planning need
entries.what: How much the Customer needs help designing their AI governance program: policies, review boards, risk classification, acceptable use rules. This is the consulting version of governance, distinct from needGovernance, which asks for software that enforces rules already decided.
entries.why: Many enterprises know they need governance but have no idea what theirs should say. Selling them an enforcement platform before the policy exists automates a vacuum. This field routes those Customers to advisory work where the policy gets written first.
entries.math: Scored 0 to 3 and multiplied by the BTG advisory route's needGovernancePlanning weight in scoreRoutes. Together with readinessGap, useCaseAmbiguity, needVendorSelection, and integrationComplexity it forms the advisory route's full score. It has no downstream cost effect.

entries.title: Operational readiness to own hardware
entries.what: Whether the Customer can actually run GPU infrastructure: data center space, power and cooling, staff who know inference serving, on call coverage, upgrade discipline. High means they already operate serious infrastructure well; none means the hardware would arrive with no one to keep it alive.
entries.why: The gap between wanting private AI and being able to operate it is where deployments die. A cluster nobody can run is more expensive than the token bill it replaced. This one field prevents the most common private AI failure mode by scoring the capability, not the ambition.
entries.math: This value fires in three route scores inside scoreRoutes: it adds points to the owned hardware route and the HPE Private Cloud AI route, and it subtracts from the rented GPU route through the opsReadinessFactor penalty, because a Customer ready to own has less reason to rent for validation. It also frames whether ceilingMonthly is achievable in practice, since operating cost eats into any savings versus the token bill.

entries.title: HPE platform preference
entries.what: How strong the Customer's existing preference for Hewlett Packard Enterprise is: incumbent HPE estate, standing procurement agreements, GreenLake relationships, or an explicit executive preference. This records commercial gravity, not technical merit.
entries.why: Enterprises rarely buy the abstractly best platform; they buy the platform their procurement, support contracts, and operations teams already know. Ignoring incumbency produces recommendations that die in purchasing. Making it a visible, weighted input keeps the bias declared instead of hidden.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the HPE Private Cloud AI route's hpePreference weight in scoreRoutes. It also feeds the owned hardware route through procurementFit, where hpePreference, nvidiaPreference, and amdPreference are summed (max 9) and multiplied by one weight, signaling that any strong vendor relationship eases an ownership purchase.

entries.title: NVIDIA preference
entries.what: How committed the Customer is to NVIDIA: existing CUDA investments, NVIDIA AI Enterprise licensing, staff trained on that stack, or a mandate from above. NVIDIA is the default in enterprise AI, so this often scores medium or high by inertia alone.
entries.why: The software ecosystem around a GPU matters more than the silicon. A shop with years of CUDA tooling pays a real switching cost to leave it, and platforms bundling NVIDIA hardware inherit that pull. Scoring the preference makes the ecosystem lock in a visible part of the decision.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value multiplies the HPE Private Cloud AI route's nvidiaPreference weight in scoreRoutes, since that platform ships NVIDIA inside. It also joins hpePreference and amdPreference in the owned route's procurementFit sum. GPU performance itself enters elsewhere, through the benchmark TPS used by gpusRequiredByThroughput.

entries.title: AMD preference
entries.what: How open or committed the Customer is to AMD accelerators and the ROCm software stack. Some shops choose AMD for price per gigabyte of HBM, supply availability, or an explicit multi vendor strategy; others will not touch a non CUDA stack.
entries.why: AMD is a genuine option for inference workloads where the serving stack supports it, and it can shift the hardware economics meaningfully. Recording the preference keeps the calculator from silently assuming NVIDIA everywhere and lets a real multi vendor posture earn points where it matters.
entries.math: The 0 to 3 value feeds only the owned hardware route, inside procurementFit, where the three vendor preferences are summed to a maximum of 9 and multiplied by one weight in scoreRoutes. Unlike hpePreference and nvidiaPreference it has no dedicated platform route, so its influence is entirely through ownership readiness.

entries.title: Usage confidence
entries.what: Whether the usage numbers in this scenario are estimates or measurements from real telemetry. Estimated means someone guessed users and runs per day in a meeting; measured means a pilot or production system produced the numbers.
entries.why: Every downstream figure inherits the quality of the usage inputs, because monthlyRuns multiplies straight through tokens, cost, and GPU counts. Buying hardware against a guess is the classic sizing failure. This field makes the tool behave differently when the foundation is soft.
entries.math: When not measured, scoreRoutes adds the usageUncertainty bonus to the rented GPU validation route and applies the usageUncertaintyPenalty to the owned hardware route: rent to learn, do not buy on a guess. It also enters the overallConfidence average, adds a warning to every recommendation, and changes the recommended nextAction to running a measured pilot first.

entries.title: Token estimate confidence
entries.what: Whether the per call token counts in the anatomy section are estimates or measured from real traces. Token counts per role, planner input, worker output, and the rest, are the atoms of the whole model, and this field records whether those atoms were weighed or guessed.
entries.why: Usage and token anatomy are separate uncertainties: you can know exactly how many users you have and still be wrong by 5x on tokens per call, especially with tool schemas and retrieved context inflating inputs. Tracking them separately shows which measurement to buy first.
entries.math: This feeds the overallConfidence average that bands the whole recommendation High, Medium, or Low, and a low band is reported with its reasons on the output. It flags that inputTokensPerRun and outputTokensPerRun, and therefore roleRoutedCost, rest on estimated anatomy. It does not change route scores directly.

entries.title: Model quality confidence
entries.what: How confident you are that the model class chosen for private deployment actually handles the workload. High means the specific open model has been evaluated on the Customer's real tasks; low means the assumption that a 70B class model is good enough has never been tested.
entries.why: Private inference sizing computes how many GPUs a model needs, but never whether that model is smart enough. A perfectly sized cluster serving an inadequate model is the most precise possible waste of money. This field keeps the quality question attached to the capacity answer.
entries.math: It enters the overallConfidence average alongside usage, token estimate, pricing, quote, benchmark, policy, and ops readiness confidence; low values drag the band down and appear in the stated reasons. It complements needQuality on the demand side and the benchmark inputs that gate gpusRequiredByThroughput on the supply side.

entries.title: Ops readiness confidence
entries.what: How confident you are in the opsReadiness rating itself. A Customer can claim strong operational maturity in a meeting; this field records whether that claim was verified, through site visits, staffing review, or a track record running comparable infrastructure.
entries.why: Self reported readiness is the least reliable input in an infrastructure deal, and it is also the one that determines whether an ownership recommendation survives contact with reality. Separating the rating from the confidence in the rating lets both be honest at once.
entries.math: It feeds the overallConfidence average that bands the recommendation and lists its weak inputs as reasons. It does not change route scores; opsReadiness moves the owned, HPE, and rented GPU scores, while this field only qualifies how much to trust that movement.

entries.title: VM count
entries.what: The number of virtual machines the cluster must host. Count everything that will actually live on the platform, including the forgotten utility servers and the test environments that never get turned off. This is the population the whole sizing exercise feeds and clothes.
entries.why: Every resource demand in the sizer scales linearly from this number, so an undercount here undercounts everything. Real estates are routinely 20 to 30 percent larger than the spreadsheet says, because discovery misses machines. Get this number from an actual inventory scan, not from memory.
entries.math: It multiplies into two demand lines: coresDemand (vmCount times avgVcpuPerVm divided by the overcommit ratio, grown by growthFactor) and ramDemandGb (vmCount times avgRamGbPerVm times growthFactor). Those set the nodesByCpu and nodesByRam gates, and the largest gate plus the failure reserve becomes nodeFloor.

entries.title: Average vCPU per VM
entries.what: The mean number of virtual CPUs assigned per VM across the estate. A vCPU is a scheduling claim on a physical core, not a dedicated core; many vCPUs share each physical core through the hypervisor scheduler. Typical general estates average 2 to 4.
entries.why: Together with vmCount this defines total vCPU demand, the raw material of CPU sizing. Averages hide monsters: one 64 vCPU database in a fleet of 2 vCPU web servers skews the mean, so check the distribution before trusting it. Oversized VMs are common and inflate this number beyond real need.
entries.math: It multiplies vmCount in the coresDemand formula, then the vCPU total is divided by the vcpuToPcpu overcommit ratio and grown by growthFactor to get physical core demand. That demand, divided by usable cores per node after the CVM tax, produces nodesByCpu, one of the three gates under nodeFloor.

entries.title: Average RAM GB per VM
entries.what: The mean memory allocation per VM in gigabytes. Unlike CPU, memory in production virtualization is sized to the actual working set with no aggressive overcommit, because reclaiming memory from a running guest hurts in ways stolen CPU cycles do not.
entries.why: RAM is very often the binding gate in real clusters: nodes run out of memory long before they run out of cores or disk. An honest average here, taken from current allocations, decides whether the cluster breathes or swaps. The field guide's rule is size to the working set, period.
entries.math: It multiplies vmCount and growthFactor in ramDemandGb. That demand is divided by usable RAM per node, which is nodeRamGb minus the CVM's reserved RAM, times the 80 percent ramCeiling, and rounded up into nodesByRam. If nodesByRam wins the gate comparison, memory is what you are buying nodes for.

entries.title: Used storage TB
entries.what: The logical data actually written today, in terabytes, before replication or efficiency. This is what the guest operating systems report as used, not the raw capacity of the current arrays and not what was provisioned. Thin provisioned estates often show provisioned numbers triple the used ones.
entries.why: Sizing from provisioned or raw numbers double counts protection overhead the new platform applies differently, so used logical data is the only honest starting point. It is also the number growth actually acts on; empty provisioned space does not grow, data does.
entries.math: It multiplies growthFactor to produce storageDemandTb, the logical data at the end of the sizing window. That demand divided by effectiveTbPerNode, the per node capacity after RF, reservation, the 75 percent ceiling, compression, and dedup, gives nodesByStorage, the third gate feeding nodeFloor.

entries.title: Growth percent per year
entries.what: The annual growth rate of the estate, applied to compute, memory, and storage demand alike. Twenty percent is a common planning default; data heavy or acquisition prone Customers run higher. It compounds, so the rate matters more than it looks.
entries.why: A cluster sized to today is full on delivery day plus one budget cycle, which is the most expensive kind of full because expansion approvals take months. The field guide's position is to size for known growth over a bounded window rather than guess at forever.
entries.math: It sets the base of growthFactor, computed as one plus the annual rate compounded over growthWindowMonths divided by 12. That single factor multiplies storageDemandTb, coresDemand, and ramDemandGb, so it scales all three node gates together before nodeFloor picks the biggest.

entries.title: Growth window months
entries.what: How far into the future the cluster is sized before planned expansion takes over. The field guide's rule is 18 to 24 months: long enough to survive procurement cycles, short enough that you are not buying hardware for demand three years of technology change away.
entries.why: Windows that are too short force emergency expansions; windows that are too long buy depreciating hardware for speculative demand. One strength of hyperconverged platforms is incremental node expansion, so the window can stay disciplined and the platform can grow when reality reports in.
entries.math: It is the exponent in growthFactor: the annual growth rate raised to growthWindowMonths divided by 12. Stretching the window from 12 to 24 months at 20 percent annual growth moves the factor from 1.20 to 1.44, and every demand line and node gate scales with it into nodeFloor.

entries.title: vCPU to pCPU ratio
entries.what: The overcommit ratio: how many virtual CPUs are scheduled per physical core. At 4:1, four vCPUs share each core, which works because most VMs idle most of the time. Field practice runs 4:1 for general workloads, 2:1 for databases, and 1:1 where latency cannot tolerate scheduling contention.
