entries.why: This is the single biggest lever in CPU sizing, and the vendor guide deliberately publishes no number because the safe ratio depends entirely on workload behavior. Doubling the ratio halves the CPU driven node count, which is exactly why optimistic ratios are how undersized clusters get sold. The values here are stated field heuristics, not vendor gospel.
entries.math: It is the divisor in coresDemand: vmCount times avgVcpuPerVm divided by this ratio, times growthFactor. A smaller ratio means more physical cores demanded, pushing nodesByCpu up, and if CPU becomes the binding gate it sets nodeFloor. The workloadType preset patches this value automatically.

entries.title: Compression ratio
entries.what: The expected data reduction from inline compression, expressed as a multiplier on effective capacity. A 1.75 ratio means the platform stores 1.75 TB of logical data per TB consumed. Real ratios depend on the data: text and databases compress well, media and encrypted data barely at all.
entries.why: Marketing decks quote 4x to 6x combined efficiency; the field guide says never quote that, because planning on best case efficiency builds a cluster that fills early. Conservative planning ratios, 1.75 for general workloads and 1.5 for databases here, are validated only by a proof of concept on the Customer's real data.
entries.math: It multiplies inside effectiveTbPerNode alongside nodeRawTb, usableMultiplier, the 75 percent storageCeiling, and dedupRatio. A higher ratio raises effective capacity per node, lowering nodesByStorage. The QA anchor shows the shape: 108 TB usable becomes roughly 216 TB effective at 2x compression.

entries.title: Dedup ratio
entries.what: The expected reduction from deduplication, which stores identical data blocks once no matter how many VMs contain them. General workloads share little, so the honest planning default is 1.0, meaning no credit. Nonpersistent VDI is the famous exception: hundreds of near identical desktops can dedup 3x or better.
entries.why: Dedup is the most oversold number in storage sizing because the spectacular VDI results get quoted against workloads that will never see them. Claiming dedup credit on a general estate is how clusters end up full a year early. Take credit only where the workload structurally earns it.
entries.math: It is the final multiplier in effectiveTbPerNode, applied after RF, reservation, the storage ceiling, and compression. Raising it from 1.0 to 3.0 (the VDI preset) triples effective per node capacity and slashes nodesByStorage. The workloadType preset sets it so the credit is tied to a defensible workload claim.

entries.title: Cores per node
entries.what: Physical CPU cores in one node of the candidate hardware model, counting all sockets. A dual socket node with two 16 core processors has 32. This is real silicon, before hyperthreading marketing and before the platform takes its cut.
entries.why: Not all of these cores serve VMs. On a hyperconverged platform the Controller VM, the storage brain that runs on every node, reserves cores first, and honest sizing pays that tax before counting capacity. The guide then plans to only 70 percent utilization at peak so failover and spikes have somewhere to land.
entries.math: In nodesByCpu, usable cores per node are nodeCores minus the CVM vCPU reservation from the cvmProfile, times the 0.70 cpuCeiling; coresDemand divided by that, rounded up, is the CPU gate. On a 32 core node with a standard 12 vCPU CVM, only about 14 cores of the 32 are plannable capacity.

entries.title: RAM GB per node
entries.what: Physical memory per node in gigabytes on the candidate hardware, commonly 512, 768, or 1024 in current configurations. Memory is usually the most expensive line item on the node bill of materials and the hardest to add later without downtime.
entries.why: Like cores, node RAM pays the Controller VM tax before serving a single guest: the standard CVM takes 48 GB per node, more under heavy storage features. The guide plans to an 80 percent ceiling so node failures and maintenance have headroom. Skipping either adjustment produces a cluster that cannot survive its first patch window.
entries.math: In nodesByRam, usable RAM per node is nodeRamGb minus the CVM RAM from the cvmProfile, times the 0.80 ramCeiling; ramDemandGb divided by that, rounded up, is the RAM gate. On a 768 GB node with a standard CVM that is 576 GB of plannable memory, and this gate very often wins nodeFloor.

entries.title: Raw TB per node
entries.what: The raw disk capacity of one node: drive count times drive size, before any protection or efficiency. A node with four 7.68 TB drives carries 30.72 TB raw. Raw is the spec sheet number, and it is the number furthest from what the Customer actually gets.
entries.why: Raw capacity shrinks through an honest gauntlet: replication cuts it roughly in half at RF2, the platform reserves 10 to 15 percent for its own operation, and planning stops at 75 percent utilization. The guide's worked example turns 245.76 TB raw into about 108 TB usable. Quoting raw as usable is the classic sizing lie.
entries.math: It is the first term of effectiveTbPerNode: nodeRawTb times usableMultiplier (the RF factor times one minus the reservation) times the storageCeiling, then times compression and dedup. That effective figure divides storageDemandTb to produce nodesByStorage, the storage gate under nodeFloor.

entries.title: Workload type
entries.what: A preset describing what the cluster mostly runs: general virtualization, nonpersistent VDI, or database. Each preset bundles the field heuristics that go with that workload, its overcommit ratio, compression and dedup expectations, and the Controller VM size it tends to demand.
entries.why: The dangerous sizing inputs travel in packs: a database estate needs conservative overcommit AND lower compression AND a heavy CVM, and picking them one at a time invites cherry picking the optimistic value of each. The preset applies a coherent, defensible set in one move. Every patched value stays visible and editable afterward.
entries.math: Choosing a preset patches vcpuToPcpu, compressionRatio, dedupRatio, and cvmProfile via applyPreset: general sets 4:1, 1.75x, 1.0x, standard; VDI sets 4:1, 1.75x, 3.0x, heavy; database sets 2:1, 1.5x, 1.0x, heavy. Those values then flow through coresDemand, effectiveTbPerNode, and both CVM taxed node gates into nodeFloor.

entries.title: Replication factor
entries.what: How the platform protects data across nodes. RF2 keeps two copies of every block and survives one node failure; RF3 keeps three and survives two. The erasure coded options, EC X 4+1 and 4+2, protect with parity instead of full copies, returning more usable capacity in exchange for rebuild cost and larger minimum clusters.
entries.why: This choice sets both the capacity economics and the failure posture of the entire cluster. RF2 hands you roughly 50 percent of raw as usable, RF3 about 33 percent, EC X up to 80 percent. It also fixes the cluster minimums: RF2 needs at least 3 nodes, RF3 at least 5, and the guide insists on sizing N+1 or N+2 so a failure or a patch window does not fill the survivors.
entries.math: The RF factor anchors usableMultiplier (rfFactor times one minus the reservation percent), which flows into effectiveTbPerNode and the storage gate. It also injects two constants into nodeFloor: the failureReserve added on top of the biggest gate (1 node for RF2 and EC X 4+1, 2 for RF3 and EC X 4+2) and the minNodes clamp the floor can never go below.

entries.title: CVM profile
entries.what: The size of the Controller VM, the storage services machine that runs on every node in a Nutanix cluster and implements the distributed storage fabric. Light reserves 8 vCPU and 32 GB RAM per node, standard 12 and 48, heavy 16 and 64 for storage intensive or database workloads.
entries.why: The CVM is the tax every node pays before hosting its first guest, and it is the most commonly forgotten line in amateur sizings. On a 32 core, 768 GB node, a standard CVM quietly takes about a third of the cores and 6 percent of the RAM, per node, forever. Honest sizing subtracts it first; the guide also says re validate these figures against current portal specs.
entries.math: The profile's vCPU count is subtracted from nodeCores inside nodesByCpu, and its RAM is subtracted from nodeRamGb inside nodesByRam, both before the 70 and 80 percent utilization ceilings apply. Heavier profiles shrink usable capacity per node, push both gates up, and can flip which gate wins nodeFloor.

entries.title: Importing a Collector or RVTools export
entries.what: Nutanix Collector and RVTools are the two standard discovery tools for virtualized estates. Collector (a free Nutanix desktop app) reads Prism, vCenter, or Hyper-V and writes a 22 sheet .xlsx; its vmList sheet is the consolidated VM inventory Nutanix Sizer itself consumes. RVTools does the same for VMware environments with sheets like vInfo and vPartition. Drop either file here and the sizer reads the estate for you.
entries.why: Typing an estate from memory is the biggest source of error in a sizing conversation. The Customer's own export replaces four guessed inputs (VM count, average vCPU, average RAM, used storage) with measured ones. The file is parsed entirely in this browser: nothing uploads, nothing is stored, and you can say that out loud in front of the Customer.
entries.math: VM count = powered-on rows minus templates, SRM placeholders, and rows Collector marks not for sizing. Averages are means across the counted VMs. Memory: MiB / 1024 shown as GB (memory hardware is binary). Storage: consumed MiB x 1.048576e-6 = decimal TB (storage is sold decimal). Consumed is used, not provisioned: thin-provisioned empty space is not demand.
